title: University of California, Berkeley

infobox.value: * [AAU](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") * [APRU](/wiki/Association_of_Pacific_Rim_Universities "Association of Pacific Rim Universities") * [IARU](/wiki/International_Alliance_of_Research_Universities "International Alliance of Research Universities") * [URA](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association") * [Space-grant](/wiki/National_Space_Grant_College_and_Fellowship_Program "National Space Grant College and Fellowship Program")

* [NCAA Division I FBS](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_Football_Bowl_Subdivision "NCAA Division I Football Bowl Subdivision") – [ACC](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") * [MPSF](/wiki/Mountain_Pacific_Sports_Federation "Mountain Pacific Sports Federation") * [IRA](/wiki/Intercollegiate_Rowing_Association "Intercollegiate Rowing Association")

(**UC Berkeley**, **Berkeley**, **Cal**, or **California**)[[10]](#cite_note-Trademark-10)[[11]](#cite_note-BrandManual-11) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [land-grant](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in the [Southside](/wiki/Southside,_Berkeley,_California "Southside, Berkeley, California") and [Northside](/wiki/Northside,_Berkeley,_California "Northside, Berkeley, California") neighborhoods of [Berkeley, California](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California"), United States. Founded in 1868 and named after the [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish "Anglo-Irish") philosopher [George Berkeley](/wiki/George_Berkeley "George Berkeley"), it is the state's first [land-grant university](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") and the founding campus of the [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California") system.[[12]](#cite_note-UCBDiscoveries-12)

The **University of California, Berkeley** (**UC Berkeley**, **Berkeley**, **Cal**, or **California**)[[10]](#cite_note-Trademark-10)[[11]](#cite_note-BrandManual-11) is a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [land-grant](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in the [Southside](/wiki/Southside,_Berkeley,_California "Southside, Berkeley, California") and [Northside](/wiki/Northside,_Berkeley,_California "Northside, Berkeley, California") neighborhoods of [Berkeley, California](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California"), United States. Founded in 1868 and named after the [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish "Anglo-Irish") philosopher [George Berkeley](/wiki/George_Berkeley "George Berkeley"), it is the state's first [land-grant university](/wiki/Land-grant_university "Land-grant university") and the founding campus of the [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California") system.[[12]](#cite_note-UCBDiscoveries-12)

Berkeley has an enrollment of more than 45,000 students. The university is organized around fifteen schools of study on the same campus, including the [College of Chemistry](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Chemistry "UC Berkeley College of Chemistry"), the [College of Engineering](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Engineering "UC Berkeley College of Engineering"), [College of Letters and Science](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Letters_and_Science "UC Berkeley College of Letters and Science"), and the [Haas School of Business](/wiki/Haas_School_of_Business "Haas School of Business"). It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity".[[13]](#cite_note-Carnegie-13) [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory") was originally founded as part of the university.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Berkeley was a founding member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities") and was one of the original eight "[Public Ivy](/wiki/Public_Ivy "Public Ivy")" schools. In 2021, the federal funding for campus research and development exceeded $1 billion.[[15]](#cite_note-R&D2-15) Thirty-two libraries also compose the [Berkeley library system](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley_Libraries "University of California, Berkeley Libraries") which is the [sixth largest research library by number of volumes held](/wiki/List_of_largest_libraries_in_the_United_States#Largest_research_libraries "List of largest libraries in the United States") in the United States.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Berkeley students compete in thirty [varsity](/wiki/Varsity_team "Varsity team") athletic sports, and the university is one of eighteen full-member institutions in the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") (ACC). Berkeley's athletic teams, the [California Golden Bears](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears "California Golden Bears"), have also won 107 national championships, 196 individual national titles, and [223 Olympic medals](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medals "List of American universities with Olympic medals") (including 121 gold).[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Berkeley's [alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_California,_Berkeley_alumni "List of University of California, Berkeley alumni"), [faculty, and researchers](/wiki/List_of_University_of_California,_Berkeley_faculty "List of University of California, Berkeley faculty") include 63 [Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_by_university_affiliation "List of Nobel laureates by university affiliation")[[21]](#cite_note-21) and 19 [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award") winners,[[22]](#cite_note-Berkeley_Law_Alumni22-22) and the university is also a producer of [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) [Marshall Scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholarship "Marshall Scholarship"),[[24]](#cite_note-24) and [Fulbright Scholars](/wiki/Fulbright_Program "Fulbright Program").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/CampanileMtTamalpiasSunset-original.jpg/250px-CampanileMtTamalpiasSunset-original.jpg)](/wiki/File:CampanileMtTamalpiasSunset-original.jpg)

Main article: [History of the University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/History_of_the_University_of_California,_Berkeley "History of the University of California, Berkeley")

### Founding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding")]

Made possible by [President Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln")'s signing of the [Morrill Act](/wiki/Morrill_Act "Morrill Act") in 1862, the University of California was founded in 1868 as the state's first land-grant university, inheriting the land and facilities of the private [College of California](/wiki/College_of_California "College of California") and the federal-funding eligibility of a public agricultural, mining, and mechanical arts college.[[26]](#cite_note-Stadtman-26) The Organic Act states that the "University shall have for its design, to provide instruction and thorough and complete education in all departments of science, literature and art, industrial and professional pursuits, and general education, and also special courses of instruction in preparation for the professions."[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Ten faculty members and forty male students made up the fledgling university when it opened in Oakland in 1869.[[29]](#cite_note-29) [Frederick Billings](/wiki/Frederick_H._Billings "Frederick H. Billings"), a trustee of the College of California, suggested that a new campus site north of Oakland be named in honor of [Anglo-Irish](/wiki/Anglo-Irish "Anglo-Irish") philosopher [George Berkeley](/wiki/George_Berkeley "George Berkeley").[[30]](#cite_note-Berkeley-30) The university began admitting women the following year.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In 1870, [Henry Durant](/wiki/Henry_Durant "Henry Durant"), founder of the College of California, became its first president. With the completion of North and [South Halls](/wiki/South_Hall_(UC_Berkeley) "South Hall (UC Berkeley)") in 1873, the university relocated to its Berkeley location with 167 male and 22 female students.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-Sunsite-33) The first female student to graduate was in 1874, admitted in the first class to include women in 1870.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Beginning in 1891, [Phoebe Apperson Hearst](/wiki/Phoebe_Apperson_Hearst "Phoebe Apperson Hearst") funded several programs and new buildings and, in 1898, sponsored an international competition in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), where French architect [Émile Bénard](/wiki/%C3%89mile_B%C3%A9nard "Émile Bénard") submitted the winning design for a campus master plan.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Robert_McNamara_official_portrait.jpg/250px-Robert_McNamara_official_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Robert_McNamara_official_portrait.jpg)

[Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara"), BA 1937

In 1905, the University Farm was established near [Sacramento](/wiki/Sacramento "Sacramento"), ultimately becoming the [University of California, Davis](/wiki/University_of_California,_Davis "University of California, Davis").[[35]](#cite_note-ucb_about-35) In 1919, the Los Angeles branch of the [California State Normal School](/wiki/California_State_Normal_School "California State Normal School") became the southern branch of the university, which ultimately became the [University of California, Los Angeles](/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles").[[36]](#cite_note-36) By the 1920s, the number of campus buildings in Berkeley had grown substantially and included twenty structures designed by architect [John Galen Howard](/wiki/John_Galen_Howard "John Galen Howard").[[37]](#cite_note-37) In 1917, one of the nation's first [ROTC](/wiki/ROTC "ROTC") programs was established at Berkeley[[38]](#cite_note-38) and its School of Military Aeronautics began training pilots, including [Jimmy Doolittle](/wiki/Jimmy_Doolittle "Jimmy Doolittle"). In 1926, future [Fleet Admiral](/wiki/Fleet_Admiral_(United_States) "Fleet Admiral (United States)") [Chester W. Nimitz](/wiki/Chester_W._Nimitz "Chester W. Nimitz") established the first [Naval ROTC](/wiki/Naval_Reserve_Officers_Training_Corps "Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps") unit at Berkeley.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Berkeley ROTC alumni include former Secretary of Defense [Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara"), Army Chief of Staff [Frederick C. Weyand](/wiki/Frederick_C._Weyand "Frederick C. Weyand"), sixteen other [general officers](/wiki/General_officers_in_the_United_States "General officers in the United States"), ten Navy [flag officers](/wiki/Flag_officer "Flag officer"), and AFROTC alumna Captain [Theresa Claiborne](/wiki/Theresa_Claiborne "Theresa Claiborne").[[40]](#cite_note-40)
In the 1930s, [Ernest Orlando Lawrence](/wiki/Ernest_Lawrence "Ernest Lawrence") helped establish the Radiation Laboratory (now [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory")) and invented the [cyclotron](/wiki/Cyclotron "Cyclotron"), which won him the Nobel physics prize in 1939.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Using the cyclotron, Berkeley professors and Berkeley Lab researchers went on to discover sixteen [chemical elements](/wiki/Chemical_elements "Chemical elements")—more than any other university in the world.[[42]](#cite_note-LBL_elements-42)[[43]](#cite_note-Periodic_table-43) In particular, during World War II and following [Glenn Seaborg](/wiki/Glenn_T._Seaborg "Glenn T. Seaborg")'s then-secret discovery of plutonium, Lawrence's Radiation Laboratory began to contract with the U.S. Army to develop the atomic bomb. Physics professor [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/Robert_Oppenheimer "Robert Oppenheimer") was named scientific head of the Manhattan Project in 1942.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) Along with the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Berkeley founded and was then a partner in managing two other labs, [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory") (1943) and [Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Livermore_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory") (1952).

The "Bodies Upon the Gears" speech (also known as "Operation of the Machine") given by [Mario Savio](/wiki/Mario_Savio "Mario Savio") on the steps of Sproul Hall in 1964

In 1952, the [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California") reorganized itself into a system of semi-autonomous campuses, with each campus given a chancellor, and [Clark Kerr](/wiki/Clark_Kerr "Clark Kerr") became Berkeley's first chancellor, while [Robert Sproul](/wiki/Robert_Gordon_Sproul "Robert Gordon Sproul") remained in place as the president of the University of California.[[46]](#cite_note-Presidents-46) Berkeley gained a worldwide reputation for [political activism](/wiki/1960s_Berkeley_protests "1960s Berkeley protests") in the 1960s. In 1964, the [Free Speech Movement](/wiki/Free_Speech_Movement "Free Speech Movement") organized student resistance to the university's restrictions on political activities on campus—most conspicuously, student activities related to the [Civil Rights Movement](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Movement "Civil Rights Movement").[[47]](#cite_note-Berkeley_60s-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The arrest in Sproul Plaza of [Jack Weinberg](/wiki/Jack_Weinberg "Jack Weinberg"), a recent Berkeley alumnus and chair of Campus [CORE](/wiki/Congress_of_Racial_Equality "Congress of Racial Equality"), prompted a series of student-led acts of formal remonstrance and civil disobedience that ultimately gave rise to the Free Speech Movement, which movement would prevail and serve as a precedent for student [opposition](/wiki/Opposition_to_United_States_involvement_in_the_Vietnam_War "Opposition to United States involvement in the Vietnam War") to America's involvement in the Vietnam War.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) In 1982, the [Mathematical Sciences Research Institute](/wiki/Mathematical_Sciences_Research_Institute "Mathematical Sciences Research Institute") (MSRI) was established on campus with support from the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation") and at the request of three Berkeley mathematicians—[Shiing-Shen Chern](/wiki/Shiing-Shen_Chern "Shiing-Shen Chern"), [Calvin Moore](/wiki/Calvin_C._Moore "Calvin C. Moore"), and [Isadore M. Singer](/wiki/Isadore_Singer "Isadore Singer"). The institute is now widely regarded as a leading center for collaborative mathematical research, drawing thousands of visiting researchers from around the world each year.[[52]](#cite_note-MSRI_history-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 21st century")]

The school has become more focused on [STEM](/wiki/Science,_technology,_engineering,_and_mathematics "Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics") disciplines and fundraising.[[55]](#cite_note-FundBerkeley-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) In 2007, the [Energy Biosciences Institute](/wiki/Energy_Biosciences_Institute "Energy Biosciences Institute") was established with funding from [BP](/wiki/BP "BP") and Stanley Hall, a research facility and headquarters for the [California Institute for Quantitative Biosciences](/wiki/California_Institute_for_Quantitative_Biosciences "California Institute for Quantitative Biosciences"), opened. Supported by a grant from alumnus [Jim Simons](/wiki/Jim_Simons "Jim Simons"), the [Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing](/wiki/Simons_Institute_for_the_Theory_of_Computing "Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing") was established in 2012. In 2015, Berkeley and its sister campus, [UCSF](/wiki/UCSF "UCSF"), established the [Innovative Genomics Institute](/wiki/Innovative_Genomics_Institute "Innovative Genomics Institute") to develop [CRISPR gene editing](/wiki/CRISPR_gene_editing "CRISPR gene editing"), and, in 2020, an anonymous donor pledged $252 million to help fund a new center for computing and data science. For the 2020 fiscal year, Berkeley set a fundraising record, receiving over $1 billion in gifts and pledges, and two years later, it broke that record, raising over $1.2 billion.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[55]](#cite_note-FundBerkeley-55)[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) In 2024, [protests](/wiki/2024_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses "2024 pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses") at Berkeley regarding the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war") resulted in police action.[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Controversies")]

* Various research ethics, human rights, and animal rights advocates have been in conflict with Berkeley. [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") contended with the school over repatriation of remains from the [Phoebe A. Hearst Museum of Anthropology](/wiki/Phoebe_A._Hearst_Museum_of_Anthropology "Phoebe A. Hearst Museum of Anthropology").[[64]](#cite_note-64) Student activists have urged the university to cut financial ties with [Tyson Foods](/wiki/Tyson_Foods "Tyson Foods") and [PepsiCo](/wiki/PepsiCo "PepsiCo").[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) Faculty member [Ignacio Chapela](/wiki/Ignacio_Chapela "Ignacio Chapela") prominently criticized the university's financial ties to [Novartis](/wiki/Novartis "Novartis").[[68]](#cite_note-68) [PETA](/wiki/PETA "PETA") has challenged the university's use of animals for research and argued that it may violate the [Animal Welfare Act](/wiki/Animal_Welfare_Act_of_1966 "Animal Welfare Act of 1966").[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)
* Cal's [Memorial Stadium](/wiki/California_Memorial_Stadium "California Memorial Stadium") reopened in September 2012 after renovations. The university incurred a controversial $445 million of debt for the stadium and a new $153 million student athletic center, which it financed with the sale of special stadium endowment seats.[[71]](#cite_note-71) The roughly $18 million interest-only annual payments on the debt consumes 20 percent of Cal's athletics' budget; principal repayment begins in 2032 and is scheduled to conclude in 2113.[[72]](#cite_note-Debt-72)
* On May 1, 2014, Berkeley was named one of fifty-five higher education institutions under investigation by the [U.S. Department of Education](/wiki/U.S._Department_of_Education "U.S. Department of Education")'s [Office of Civil Rights](/wiki/Office_of_Civil_Rights "Office of Civil Rights") "for possible violations of federal law over the handling of sexual violence and harassment complaints" by the [White House Task Force to Protect Students from Sexual Assault](/wiki/White_House_Task_Force_to_Protect_Students_from_Sexual_Assault "White House Task Force to Protect Students from Sexual Assault").[[73]](#cite_note-73) Investigations continued into 2016, with hundreds of pages of records released in April 2016, showing a pattern of documented sexual harassment and firings of non-tenured staff.[[74]](#cite_note-74)
* On July 25, 2019, Berkeley was removed from the [*U.S. News* Best Colleges Ranking](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report_Best_Colleges_Ranking "U.S. News & World Report Best Colleges Ranking") for misreporting statistics. Berkeley had originally reported that its two-year average alumni giving rate for fiscal years 2017 and 2016 was 11.6 percent, *[U.S. News](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* said. The school later told *U.S. News* the correct average alumni giving rate for the 2016 fiscal year was just 7.9 percent. The school incorrectly overstated its alumni giving data to *U.S. News* since at least 2014. The alumni giving rate accounts for five percent of the Best Colleges ranking.[[75]](#cite_note-75)
* Berkeley community members have criticized UC Berkeley's increasing enrollment. Berkeley residents filed a lawsuit alleging that the university's expanding enrollment violated [California Environmental Quality Act](/wiki/California_Environmental_Quality_Act "California Environmental Quality Act") and that the area lacked the infrastructure to support more students.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Critics of the lawsuit accused these community members of [NIMBYism](/wiki/NIMBYism "NIMBYism").[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79) In August 2021, a judge from the [Superior Court of Alameda County](/wiki/Superior_Court_of_Alameda_County "Superior Court of Alameda County") ruled in favor of the residents, and on March 3, 2022, the [California Supreme Court](/wiki/California_Supreme_Court "California Supreme Court") also ruled in favor of the residents, saying that the university needed to freeze its admission rates at 2020–2021 levels.[[80]](#cite_note-80) On March 11, 2022, state legislators released a proposal to change CEQA to exempt the university from its restrictions.[[81]](#cite_note-81) On March 14, Gavin Newsom signed the bill into law.[[82]](#cite_note-82) Berkeley has continued to face a housing shortage.[[83]](#cite_note-83)
* In February 2025, [Leo Terrell](/wiki/Leo_Terrell "Leo Terrell"), the head of the Trump administration's [Task Force to Combat Antisemitism](/wiki/Antisemitism_in_the_United_States#Efforts_to_combat_antisemitism "Antisemitism in the United States"), announced that he would investigate Berkeley as part of the Department of Justice's broader investigation into [antisemitism on college campuses](/wiki/Universities_and_antisemitism "Universities and antisemitism").[[84]](#cite_note-84)
* In 2026, Russian authorities declared the university an [undesirable organization](/wiki/Russian_undesirable_organizations_law "Russian undesirable organizations law").[[85]](#cite_note-85)

## Organization and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Organization and administration")]

### Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Name")]

Officially named the "University of California, Berkeley" it is often shortened to "Berkeley" in general reference or in an academic context ([Berkeley Law](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Law "UC Berkeley School of Law"), [Berkeley Engineering](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Engineering "UC Berkeley College of Engineering"), [Berkeley Haas](/wiki/Haas_School_of_Business "Haas School of Business"), [Berkeley Public Health](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Public_Health "UC Berkeley School of Public Health")) and to "California" or "Cal" particularly when referring to its athletic teams ([California Golden Bears](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears "California Golden Bears")).[[10]](#cite_note-Trademark-10)[[11]](#cite_note-BrandManual-11)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Governance")]

The University of California is governed by a twenty-six member [Board of Regents](/wiki/Regents_of_the_University_of_California "Regents of the University of California"), eighteen of whom are appointed by the [Governor of California](/wiki/Governor_of_California "Governor of California") to 12-year terms. The board also has seven *[ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio "Ex officio")* members, a student regent, and a non-voting student regent-designate.[[87]](#cite_note-Governance-87) Prior to 1952, Berkeley was the University of California, so the university president was also Berkeley's chief executive. In 1952, the university reorganized itself into a system of semi-autonomous campuses, with each campus having its own chief executive, a chancellor, who would, in turn, report to the president of the university system. Twelve vice-chancellors report directly to Berkeley's chancellor, and the deans of the fifteen colleges and schools report to the executive vice chancellor and provost, Berkeley's chief academic officer.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Twenty-three presidents and chancellors have led Berkeley since its founding.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[46]](#cite_note-Presidents-46)

### Funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Funding")]

See also: [University of California finances](/wiki/University_of_California_finances "University of California finances")

With the exception of government contracts, public support is apportioned to Berkeley and the other campuses of the University of California system through the UC Office of the President and accounts for 12 percent of Berkeley's total revenues.[[90]](#cite_note-90) Berkeley has benefited from private philanthropy and alumni and their foundations have given to the university for operations and capital expenditures with the more prominent being [J. Paul Getty](/wiki/J._Paul_Getty "J. Paul Getty"), [Ann Getty](/wiki/Ann_Getty "Ann Getty"), [Sanford Diller](/wiki/Sanford_Diller "Sanford Diller"), [Donald Fisher](/wiki/Donald_Fisher "Donald Fisher"), [Flora Lamson Hewlett](/wiki/Flora_Lamson_Hewlett "Flora Lamson Hewlett"), David Schwartz ([Bio-Rad](/wiki/Bio-Rad "Bio-Rad")) and members of the Haas ([Walter A. Haas](/wiki/Walter_A._Haas "Walter A. Haas"), [Rhoda Haas Goldman](/wiki/Rhoda_Haas_Goldman "Rhoda Haas Goldman"), [Walter A. Haas Jr.](/wiki/Walter_A._Haas_Jr. "Walter A. Haas Jr."), [Peter E. Haas](/wiki/Peter_E._Haas "Peter E. Haas"), [Bob Haas](/wiki/Bob_Haas "Bob Haas")) family.[[91]](#cite_note-IHE-91)

Berkeley has also benefited from benefactors beyond its alumni ranks, notable among which are [Mark Zuckerberg](/wiki/Mark_Zuckerberg "Mark Zuckerberg") and [Priscilla Chan](/wiki/Priscilla_Chan "Priscilla Chan"); [Vitalik Buterin](/wiki/Vitalik_Buterin "Vitalik Buterin"), [Patrick Collison](/wiki/Patrick_Collison "Patrick Collison"), [John Collison](/wiki/John_Collison "John Collison"), the [Ron Conway](/wiki/Ron_Conway "Ron Conway") family, [Daniel Gross](/wiki/Daniel_Gross_(software_entrepreneur) "Daniel Gross (software entrepreneur)"), [Dustin Moskovitz](/wiki/Dustin_Moskovitz "Dustin Moskovitz") and [Cari Tuna](/wiki/Cari_Tuna "Cari Tuna"), along with [Jane Street](/wiki/Jane_Street_Capital "Jane Street Capital") principals; [BP](/wiki/BP "BP"); the Bill and Melinda Gates Foundation, billionaire [Sir Li Ka-Shing](/wiki/Li_Ka-Shing "Li Ka-Shing"), Israeli-Russian billionaire [Yuri Milner](/wiki/Yuri_Milner "Yuri Milner"), [Thomas and Stacey Siebel](/wiki/Thomas_Siebel "Thomas Siebel"), [Sanford and Joan Weill](/wiki/Sanford_I._Weill "Sanford I. Weill"), and professor [Gordon Rausser](/wiki/Gordon_Rausser "Gordon Rausser") ($50 million gift in 2020).[[91]](#cite_note-IHE-91) Hundreds of millions of dollars have been given anonymously.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The 2008–13 "Campaign for Berkeley" raised $3.13 billion from 281,855 donors, and the "Light the Way" campaign, which concluded at the end of 2023, has raised over $6.2 billion.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Academics")]

### Faculty and departments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Faculty and departments")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Wheeler_Hall%2C_University_of_California%2C_Berkeley.jpg/250px-Wheeler_Hall%2C_University_of_California%2C_Berkeley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wheeler_Hall,_University_of_California,_Berkeley.jpg)

[Wheeler Hall](/wiki/Wheeler_Hall "Wheeler Hall"), home to Berkeley's largest lecture hall, was the location of a [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") conferral during [WWII](/wiki/WWII "WWII").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Hearst_Mining_Building_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg/250px-Hearst_Mining_Building_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hearst_Mining_Building_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg)

The interior of the [Hearst Mining Building](/wiki/Hearst_Memorial_Mining_Building "Hearst Memorial Mining Building"), dedicated by [Phoebe Hearst](/wiki/Phoebe_Hearst "Phoebe Hearst") in honor of her late husband, [George](/wiki/George_Hearst "George Hearst")

Berkeley is a large, primarily residential research university with a majority of its enrollment in undergraduate programs but also offering a comprehensive doctoral program.[[13]](#cite_note-Carnegie-13) The university has been [accredited](/wiki/Educational_accreditation "Educational accreditation") by the [Western Association of Schools and Colleges](/wiki/Western_Association_of_Schools_and_Colleges "Western Association of Schools and Colleges") Senior College and University Commission since 1949.[[94]](#cite_note-WASC-94) The university operates on a [semester calendar](/wiki/Academic_term "Academic term") and awarded 8,725 bachelor's, 3,286 master's or professional and 1,272 doctoral degrees in 2018–2019.[[95]](#cite_note-CDS2019-95)

There are 1,789 full-time and 886 part-time faculty members among the university's academic enterprise which is organized into fifteen colleges and schools that comprise 180 departments and 80 interdisciplinary units offering over 350 degree programs. Colleges serve both undergraduate and graduate students, while schools are generally graduate only, though some offer undergraduate majors or minors:

* [College of Chemistry](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Chemistry "UC Berkeley College of Chemistry")
* [College of Computing, Data Science, and Society](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Computing,_Data_Science,_and_Society "UC Berkeley College of Computing, Data Science, and Society")
* [College of Engineering](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Engineering "UC Berkeley College of Engineering")
* [College of Environmental Design](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Environmental_Design "UC Berkeley College of Environmental Design")
* [College of Letters and Science](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Letters_and_Science "UC Berkeley College of Letters and Science")
* [Goldman School of Public Policy](/wiki/Goldman_School_of_Public_Policy "Goldman School of Public Policy")
* [Graduate School of Journalism](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_Graduate_School_of_Journalism "UC Berkeley Graduate School of Journalism")
* [Haas School of Business](/wiki/Haas_School_of_Business "Haas School of Business")
* [Rausser College of Natural Resources](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_College_of_Natural_Resources "UC Berkeley College of Natural Resources")
* [School of Information](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Information "UC Berkeley School of Information")
* [School of Education](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Education "UC Berkeley School of Education")
* [School of Law](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Law "UC Berkeley School of Law")
* [School of Public Health](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Public_Health "UC Berkeley School of Public Health")
* [School of Social Welfare](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Social_Welfare "UC Berkeley School of Social Welfare")
* [Wertheim School of Optometry](/wiki/Herbert_Wertheim_School_of_Optometry_and_Vision_Science "Herbert Wertheim School of Optometry and Vision Science")
* [UC Berkeley Extension](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_Extension "UC Berkeley Extension")

### Undergraduate programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Undergraduate programs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/UCB_Doe_Memorial_Library_oblique_view_dllu.jpg/250px-UCB_Doe_Memorial_Library_oblique_view_dllu.jpg)](/wiki/File:UCB_Doe_Memorial_Library_oblique_view_dllu.jpg)

[Doe Memorial Library](/wiki/Doe_Memorial_Library "Doe Memorial Library")

The four-year, full-time undergraduate program offers 107 bachelor's degrees across the Haas School of Business (1), College of Chemistry (5), College of Engineering (20), College of Environmental Design (4), College of Letters and Science (67), Rausser College of Natural Resources (10), and individual majors (2).[[96]](#cite_note-Degree_totals-96) The most popular majors are [electrical engineering and computer sciences](/wiki/Computer_science_and_engineering "Computer science and engineering"), [political science](/wiki/Political_science "Political science"), [molecular](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology") and [cell biology](/wiki/Cell_biology "Cell biology"), [environmental science](/wiki/Environmental_science "Environmental science"), and [economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics").[[97]](#cite_note-Facts-97)

Requirements for undergraduate degrees include an entry-level writing requirement before enrollment (typically fulfilled by minimum scores on standardized admissions exams such as the SAT or ACT), completing coursework on "American History and Institutions" before or after enrollment by taking an introductory class, passing an "American Cultures Breadth" class at Berkeley, as well as requirements for reading and composition and specific requirements declared by the department and school.[[98]](#cite_note-Undergrad_requirements-98)

### Graduate and professional programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Graduate and professional programs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Haas_School_of_Business_central_courtyard.JPG/250px-Haas_School_of_Business_central_courtyard.JPG)](/wiki/File:Haas_School_of_Business_central_courtyard.JPG)

[Haas School of Business](/wiki/Haas_School_of_Business "Haas School of Business")

Berkeley has a "comprehensive" graduate program, with high coexistence with the programs offered to undergraduates, and offers interdisciplinary graduate programs with the medical schools at the [University of California, San Francisco](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Francisco "University of California, San Francisco") and [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"). The university offers [Master of Arts](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts"), [Master of Science](/wiki/Master_of_Science "Master of Science"), [Master of Fine Arts](/wiki/Master_of_Fine_Arts "Master of Fine Arts"), and [PhD](/wiki/PhD "PhD") degrees in addition to professional degrees such as the [Juris Doctor](/wiki/Juris_Doctor "Juris Doctor"), [Master of Business Administration](/wiki/Master_of_Business_Administration "Master of Business Administration"), [Master of Public Health](/wiki/Master_of_Public_Health "Master of Public Health"), and [Master of Design](/wiki/Master_of_Design "Master of Design").[[13]](#cite_note-Carnegie-13)[[99]](#cite_note-99) The university awarded 963 doctoral degrees and 3,531 master's degrees in 2017.[[100]](#cite_note-Graduate_profile-100) Admission to graduate programs is decentralized; applicants apply directly to the department or degree program. Most graduate students are supported by fellowships, teaching assistantships, or research assistantships.[[100]](#cite_note-Graduate_profile-100)

### Library system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Library system")]

Main article: [University of California, Berkeley Library System](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley_Library_System "University of California, Berkeley Library System")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Berkeley_T-rex_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg/250px-Berkeley_T-rex_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Berkeley_T-rex_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg)

The on-campus [University of California Museum of Paleontology](/wiki/University_of_California_Museum_of_Paleontology "University of California Museum of Paleontology") hosts a [life-size replica](/wiki/Replica "Replica") of a [T-rex](/wiki/T-rex "T-rex").

[Doe Library](/wiki/Doe_Memorial_Library "Doe Memorial Library") serves as the [Berkeley library system](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley_Libraries "University of California, Berkeley Libraries")'s reference, periodical, and administrative center, while most of the main collections reside in the subterranean Gardner Main Stacks and Moffitt Undergraduate Library. The [Bancroft Library](/wiki/Bancroft_Library "Bancroft Library"), which has over 400,000 printed volumes and 70 million manuscripts, pictures, and maps, maintains special collections that document the history of the western part of North America, with an emphasis on California, Mexico and Central America. The Bancroft Library also houses the Mark Twain Papers,[[101]](#cite_note-101) the Oral History Center,[[102]](#cite_note-102) the Center for the Tebtunis Papyri,[[103]](#cite_note-103) and the University Archives.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

#### Historical rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Historical rankings")]

In his memoirs, [Clark Kerr](/wiki/Clark_Kerr "Clark Kerr") records Berkeley's rise in the rankings (according to the [National Academies](/wiki/National_academies "National academies")) during the 20th century. The school's first ranking in 1906 placed it among the top six schools ("Big Six") in the nation. In 1934, it ranked second, tied with [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") and the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), behind only [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"); in 1957, it was ranked as the only school second to Harvard. In 1964, Berkeley was named the "best balanced distinguished university", meaning the school had not only the most top departments but also the highest percentage of top ranking departments in its school. The school in 1993 was the only remaining member of the original 1906 "Big Six", along with Harvard; in that year Berkeley ranked first.[[124]](#cite_note-Kerr_Page_404-124)

The [American Council on Education](/wiki/American_Council_on_Education "American Council on Education"), a private non-profit association, ranked Berkeley tenth in 1934. However, by 1942, private funding had helped Berkeley rise to second place, behind only Harvard, based on the number of distinguished departments.[[46]](#cite_note-Presidents-46) In 1985, [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") admissions officer Richard Moll published *Public Ivies: A Guide to America's Best Public Undergraduate Colleges and Universities* which named Berkeley a "[Public Ivy](/wiki/Public_Ivy "Public Ivy")".[[125]](#cite_note-JBHE2-125)[[126]](#cite_note-126)[[127]](#cite_note-PublicIvys-127)[[128]](#cite_note-Greenes_2001-128)

The 2010 [United States National Research Council Rankings](/wiki/United_States_National_Research_Council_Rankings "United States National Research Council Rankings") identified Berkeley as having the highest number of top-ranked doctoral programs in the nation. Berkeley doctoral programs that received a #1 ranking included English, German, Political Science, Geography, Agricultural and Resource Economics, Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Biochemistry, Molecular Biology, Genetics, Genomics, Epidemiology, Plant Biology, Computer Science, Electrical Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, and Civil and Environmental Engineering.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

For Fall 2022, Berkeley's total enrollment was 45,745: 32,831 undergraduate and 12,914 graduate students, with women accounting for 56% of undergraduates and 49% of graduate and professional students. It had 128,226 freshman applicants and accepted 14,614 (11.4%). Among enrolled freshman, the average unweighted GPA was 3.90.[[131]](#cite_note-CDS-133)

Berkeley's enrollment of [National Merit Scholars](/wiki/National_Merit_Scholarship_Program "National Merit Scholarship Program") was third in the nation until 2002, when participation in the National Merit program was discontinued.[[132]](#cite_note-134) For 2019, Berkeley ranked fourth in enrollment of recipients of the [National Merit $2,500 Scholarship](/wiki/National_Merit_Scholarship_Program "National Merit Scholarship Program") (132 scholars).[[133]](#cite_note-135)[[134]](#cite_note-136) 27% of admitted students receive federal [Pell grants](/wiki/Pell_grant "Pell grant").[[135]](#cite_note-137)

Berkeley students are eligible for a variety of public and private financial aid. Inquiries are processed through the Financial Aid and Scholarships Office, although schools such as the Haas School of Business[[136]](#cite_note-138) and [Berkeley Law](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Law "UC Berkeley School of Law"),[[137]](#cite_note-139) have their own financial aid offices.

## Discoveries and innovation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Discoveries and innovation")]

[![A simple flow chart showing the history and timeline of the development of Unix starting with one bubble at the top and 13 tributaries at the bottom of the flow](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Unix_history-simple.svg/250px-Unix_history-simple.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Unix_history-simple.svg)

Simplified evolution of [Unix](/wiki/Unix "Unix") systems and [BSD](/wiki/BSD "BSD") [forks](/wiki/Fork_(software_development) "Fork (software development)")

### Natural sciences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Natural sciences")]

* [Atomic bomb](/wiki/Atomic_bomb "Atomic bomb") – Physics professor [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer") was wartime director of [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory") and the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project").
* [Carbon 14](/wiki/Carbon-14 "Carbon-14") and [photosynthesis](/wiki/Photosynthesis "Photosynthesis") – [Martin Kamen](/wiki/Martin_Kamen "Martin Kamen") and [Sam Ruben](/wiki/Sam_Ruben "Sam Ruben") first discovered carbon 14 in 1940, and Nobel laureate [Melvin Calvin](/wiki/Melvin_Calvin "Melvin Calvin") and his colleagues used carbon 14 as a molecular tracer to reveal the carbon assimilation path in photosynthesis, known as [Calvin cycle](/wiki/Light-independent_reactions "Light-independent reactions").[[138]](#cite_note-140)
* [Carcinogens](/wiki/Carcinogens "Carcinogens") – Identified chemicals that damage DNA. The [Ames test](/wiki/Ames_test "Ames test") was described in a series of papers in 1973 by [Bruce Ames](/wiki/Bruce_Ames "Bruce Ames") and his group at the university.
* [Chemical elements](/wiki/Chemical_element "Chemical element") – Sixteen elements have been discovered at Berkeley ([technetium](/wiki/Technetium "Technetium"), [astatine](/wiki/Astatine "Astatine"), [neptunium](/wiki/Neptunium "Neptunium"), [plutonium](/wiki/Plutonium "Plutonium"), [americium](/wiki/Americium "Americium"), [curium](/wiki/Curium "Curium"), [berkelium](/wiki/Berkelium "Berkelium"), [californium](/wiki/Californium "Californium"), [einsteinium](/wiki/Einsteinium "Einsteinium"), [fermium](/wiki/Fermium "Fermium"), [mendelevium](/wiki/Mendelevium "Mendelevium"), [nobelium](/wiki/Nobelium "Nobelium"), [lawrencium](/wiki/Lawrencium "Lawrencium"), [rutherfordium](/wiki/Rutherfordium "Rutherfordium"), [dubnium](/wiki/Dubnium "Dubnium"), and [seaborgium](/wiki/Seaborgium "Seaborgium")).[[139]](#cite_note-alumni.berkeley.edu-141)[[140]](#cite_note-llnl.gov-142)
* [Covalent bond](/wiki/Covalent_bond "Covalent bond") – [Gilbert N. Lewis](/wiki/Gilbert_N._Lewis "Gilbert N. Lewis") in 1916 described the sharing of electron pairs between atoms, and invented the Lewis notation to describe the mechanisms.
* [CRISPR gene editing](/wiki/CRISPR "CRISPR") – Nobel laureate [Jennifer Doudna](/wiki/Jennifer_Doudna "Jennifer Doudna") discovered a precise and inexpensive way for manipulating DNA in cells.[[141]](#cite_note-143)
* [Cyclotron](/wiki/Cyclotron "Cyclotron") – [Ernest O. Lawrence](/wiki/Ernest_O._Lawrence "Ernest O. Lawrence") created a [particle accelerator](/wiki/Particle_accelerator "Particle accelerator") in 1934, and was awarded the Nobel Physics Prize in 1939.[[142]](#cite_note-144)
* [Dark energy](/wiki/Dark_energy "Dark energy") – [Saul Perlmutter](/wiki/Saul_Perlmutter "Saul Perlmutter") and many others in the [Supernova Cosmology Project](/wiki/Supernova_Cosmology_Project "Supernova Cosmology Project") discover the universe is expanding because of dark energy 1998.
* [Flu vaccine](/wiki/Flu_vaccination "Flu vaccination") – [Wendell M. Stanley](/wiki/Wendell_M._Stanley "Wendell M. Stanley") and colleagues discovered the vaccine in the 1940s.
* [Hydrogen bomb](/wiki/Thermonuclear_weapon "Thermonuclear weapon") – [Edward Teller](/wiki/Edward_Teller "Edward Teller"), the father of hydrogen bomb, was a professor at Berkeley and a researcher at the [Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Livermore_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory") and the [Los Alamos National Laboratory](/wiki/Los_Alamos_National_Laboratory "Los Alamos National Laboratory").
* [Immunotherapy of cancer](/wiki/Immunotherapy_of_cancer "Immunotherapy of cancer") – [James P. Allison](/wiki/James_P._Allison "James P. Allison") discovers and develops [monoclonal antibody therapy](/wiki/Monoclonal_antibody_therapy "Monoclonal antibody therapy") that uses the immune system to combat cancer in 1992–1995.
* [Molecular clock](/wiki/Molecular_clock "Molecular clock") – [Allan Wilson](/wiki/Allan_Wilson_(biologist) "Allan Wilson (biologist)") discovered it in 1967.
* [Neuroplasticity](/wiki/Neuroplasticity "Neuroplasticity") – [Marian Diamond](/wiki/Marian_Diamond "Marian Diamond") discovers structural, biochemical, and synaptic changes in brain caused by environmental enrichment in 1964.
* [Oncogene](/wiki/Oncogene "Oncogene") – [Peter Duesberg](/wiki/Peter_Duesberg "Peter Duesberg") discovers the first cancer causing gene in a virus in the 1970s.
* [Telomerase](/wiki/Telomerase "Telomerase") – [Elizabeth H. Blackburn](/wiki/Elizabeth_H._Blackburn "Elizabeth H. Blackburn"), [Carol Greider](/wiki/Carol_Greider "Carol Greider"), and [Jack Szostak](/wiki/Jack_Szostak "Jack Szostak") discover enzyme that promotes cell division and growth 1985.
* [Vitamin E](/wiki/Vitamin_E "Vitamin E") – [Gladys Anderson Emerson](/wiki/Gladys_Anderson_Emerson "Gladys Anderson Emerson") isolates Vitamin E in a pure form in 1935.[[143]](#cite_note-EWS-145)

### Computer and applied sciences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Computer and applied sciences")]

* [Berkeley RISC](/wiki/Berkeley_RISC "Berkeley RISC") – [David Patterson](/wiki/David_Patterson_(computer_scientist) "David Patterson (computer scientist)") leads [ARPA](/wiki/DARPA "DARPA")'s [VLSI project](/wiki/VLSI_project "VLSI project") of [microprocessor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") design 1980–1984.[[144]](#cite_note-coinrisk-146)
* [Berkeley UNIX/Berkeley Software Distribution (BSD)](/wiki/Berkeley_UNIX "Berkeley UNIX") – The [Computer Systems Research Group](/wiki/Computer_Systems_Research_Group "Computer Systems Research Group") was a research group at Berkeley that was dedicated to enhancing [AT&T](/wiki/Old_AT%26T "Old AT&T") [Unix](/wiki/Unix "Unix") operating system and funded by [Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency](/wiki/Defense_Advanced_Research_Projects_Agency "Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency"). [Bill Joy](/wiki/Bill_Joy "Bill Joy") modified the code and released it in 1977 under the open source [BSD license](/wiki/BSD_license "BSD license"), starting an open-source revolution.
* [Deep sea diving](/wiki/Deep_sea_diving "Deep sea diving") – [Joel Henry Hildebrand](/wiki/Joel_Henry_Hildebrand "Joel Henry Hildebrand") used [helium](/wiki/Helium "Helium") with [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") to mitigate [decompression sickness](/wiki/Decompression_sickness "Decompression sickness").[[145]](#cite_note-147)
* [GIMP](/wiki/GIMP "GIMP") – In 1995, [Spencer Kimball](/wiki/Spencer_Kimball_(computer_programmer) "Spencer Kimball (computer programmer)") and [Peter Mattis](/wiki/Peter_Mattis "Peter Mattis") began developing GIMP as a semester-long project at Berkeley.
* [Polygraph](/wiki/Polygraph "Polygraph") – invented by [John Augustus Larson](/wiki/John_Augustus_Larson "John Augustus Larson") and a police officer from the [Berkeley Police Department](/wiki/Berkeley_Police_Department "Berkeley Police Department") in 1921.[[146]](#cite_note-148)
* [Project Genie](/wiki/Project_Genie "Project Genie") – [DARPA](/wiki/DARPA "DARPA") funded project. It produced an early [time-sharing](/wiki/Time-sharing "Time-sharing") system including the [Berkeley Timesharing System](/wiki/Berkeley_Timesharing_System "Berkeley Timesharing System"), which was then commercialized as the [SDS 940](/wiki/SDS_940 "SDS 940"). Concepts from Project Genie influenced the development of the [TENEX](/wiki/TENEX_(operating_system) "TENEX (operating system)") operating system for the [PDP-10](/wiki/PDP-10 "PDP-10"), and [Unix](/wiki/Unix "Unix"), which inherited the concept of [process forking](/wiki/Fork_(system_call) "Fork (system call)") from it.[[147]](#cite_note-Ritchie-149) Unix co-creator [Ken Thompson](/wiki/Ken_Thompson_(computer_programmer) "Ken Thompson (computer programmer)") worked on Project Genie while at Berkeley.
* [SPICE](/wiki/SPICE "SPICE") – [Donald O. Pederson](/wiki/Donald_O._Pederson "Donald O. Pederson") develops the Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis (SPICE) 1972.[[148]](#cite_note-150)
* [Tcl programming language](/wiki/Tcl_programming_language "Tcl programming language") – developed by [John Ousterhout](/wiki/John_Ousterhout "John Ousterhout") in 1988.[[149]](#cite_note-151)
* Three-dimensional [Transistor](/wiki/Transistor "Transistor") – [Chenming Hu](/wiki/Chenming_Hu "Chenming Hu") won the 2014 [National Medal of Technology](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Technology "National Medal of Technology") for developing the "first 3-dimensional transistors, which radically advanced semiconductor technology."[[150]](#cite_note-152)
* [Vi](/wiki/Vi_(text_editor) "Vi (text editor)") text editor – [Bill Joy](/wiki/Bill_Joy "Bill Joy") created the first Vi editor in 1976.[[151]](#cite_note-153)
* [Wetsuit](/wiki/Wetsuit "Wetsuit") – [Hugh Bradner](/wiki/Hugh_Bradner "Hugh Bradner") invents first wetsuit 1952.[[152]](#cite_note-154)
