## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Campus")]

Main article: [Campus of the University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/Campus_of_the_University_of_California,_Berkeley "Campus of the University of California, Berkeley")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Sather_Gate_at_University_of_California%2C_Berkeley%2C_California_LCCN2013633500_%28edited%29.jpg/250px-Sather_Gate_at_University_of_California%2C_Berkeley%2C_California_LCCN2013633500_%28edited%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sather_Gate_at_University_of_California,_Berkeley,_California_LCCN2013633500_(edited).jpg)

[Sather Gate](/wiki/Sather_Gate "Sather Gate"), connecting [Sproul Plaza](/wiki/Sproul_Plaza "Sproul Plaza") to the inner campus, was a center of the [Free Speech Movement](/wiki/Free_Speech_Movement "Free Speech Movement").

Much of the Berkeley campus is in the city limits of [Berkeley](/wiki/Berkeley,_California "Berkeley, California") with portion of the property extending into [Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California").[[171]](#cite_note-173) It encompasses approximately 1,232-acres, though the "central campus" occupies only the low-lying western 178-acres of this area. Of the remaining acres, approximately 200-acres are occupied by the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory; other facilities above the main campus include the [Lawrence Hall of Science](/wiki/Lawrence_Hall_of_Science "Lawrence Hall of Science") and several research units, notably the [Space Sciences Laboratory](/wiki/Space_Sciences_Laboratory "Space Sciences Laboratory"), the [Mathematical Sciences Research Institute](/wiki/Mathematical_Sciences_Research_Institute "Mathematical Sciences Research Institute"), an 800-acre (320-hectare) ecological preserve, the [University of California Botanical Garden](/wiki/University_of_California_Botanical_Garden "University of California Botanical Garden") and a recreation center in Strawberry Canyon. Portions of the mostly undeveloped, eastern area of the campus are actually within the [City of Oakland](/wiki/Oakland,_California "Oakland, California"); these portions extend from the [Claremont Resort](/wiki/Claremont_Resort "Claremont Resort") north through the [Panoramic Hill](/wiki/Panoramic_Hill,_Oakland/Berkeley,_California "Panoramic Hill, Oakland/Berkeley, California") neighborhood to [Tilden Park](/wiki/Tilden_Regional_Park "Tilden Regional Park").[[172]](#cite_note-174)

To the west of the central campus is the [downtown business district of Berkeley](/wiki/Downtown_Berkeley,_California "Downtown Berkeley, California"); to the northwest is the neighborhood of North Berkeley, including the so-called [Gourmet Ghetto](/wiki/Gourmet_ghetto "Gourmet ghetto"), a commercial district known for high quality dining due to the presence of such world-renowned restaurants as [Chez Panisse](/wiki/Chez_Panisse "Chez Panisse"). Immediately to the north is a quiet residential neighborhood known as [Northside](/wiki/Northside,_Berkeley,_California "Northside, Berkeley, California") with a large graduate student population;[[173]](#cite_note-berk-post-doc-175) situated north of that are the upscale residential neighborhoods of the [Berkeley Hills](/wiki/Berkeley_Hills "Berkeley Hills"). Immediately southeast of campus lies fraternity row and beyond that the [Clark Kerr Campus](/wiki/Clark_Kerr_Campus "Clark Kerr Campus") and an upscale residential area named [Claremont](/wiki/Claremont,_Oakland/Berkeley,_California "Claremont, Oakland/Berkeley, California"). The [area south of the university](/wiki/Southside,_Berkeley,_California "Southside, Berkeley, California") includes student housing and [Telegraph Avenue](/wiki/Telegraph_Avenue "Telegraph Avenue"), one of Berkeley's main shopping districts with stores, street vendors and restaurants catering to college students and tourists. In addition, the university also owns land to the northwest of the main campus, a married student housing complex in the nearby town of Albany ("Albany Village" and the "Gill Tract"), and a [field research station](/wiki/Berkeley_Global_Campus_at_Richmond_Bay "Berkeley Global Campus at Richmond Bay") several miles to the north in [Richmond, California](/wiki/Richmond,_California "Richmond, California").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Bancroft_Library_-_University_of_California%2C_Berkeley_-_DSC04902.JPG/250px-Bancroft_Library_-_University_of_California%2C_Berkeley_-_DSC04902.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bancroft_Library_-_University_of_California,_Berkeley_-_DSC04902.JPG)

[Bancroft Library](/wiki/Bancroft_Library "Bancroft Library")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Botanischer_Garten_in_Berkeley%2C_California.JPG/250px-Botanischer_Garten_in_Berkeley%2C_California.JPG)](/wiki/File:Botanischer_Garten_in_Berkeley,_California.JPG)

The [UC Botanical Garden](/wiki/UC_Botanical_Garden "UC Botanical Garden"), located in the [Berkeley Hills](/wiki/Berkeley_Hills "Berkeley Hills") and by the [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory")

The campus is home to several museums including the [University of California Museum of Paleontology](/wiki/University_of_California_Museum_of_Paleontology "University of California Museum of Paleontology"), the [Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive](/wiki/Berkeley_Art_Museum_and_Pacific_Film_Archive "Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive"), and the [Lawrence Hall of Science](/wiki/Lawrence_Hall_of_Science "Lawrence Hall of Science"). The Museum of Paleontology, found in the lobby of the Valley Life Sciences Building, showcases a variety of dinosaur fossils including a complete cast of a Tyrannosaurus Rex. The campus also offers resources for innovation and entrepreneurship, such as the Big Ideas Competition, the Sutardja Center for Entrepreneurship and Technology, and the Berkeley Haas Innovation Lab.[[174]](#cite_note-176) The campus is also home to the [University of California Botanical Garden](/wiki/University_of_California_Botanical_Garden "University of California Botanical Garden"), with more than 12,000 individual species.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/University_of_California%2C_Berkeley.jpg/1280px-University_of_California%2C_Berkeley.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_California,_Berkeley.jpg "360-degree-view of the UC Berkeley campus")

360-degree-view of the UC Berkeley campus

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Architecture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/South_Hall--UC_Berkeley--Panoramic.jpg/250px-South_Hall--UC_Berkeley--Panoramic.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_Hall--UC_Berkeley--Panoramic.jpg)

[South Hall](/wiki/South_Hall_(UC_Berkeley) "South Hall (UC Berkeley)") (1873), one of the two original buildings of the University of California, still stands on the Berkeley campus.

What is considered the historic campus today was the result of the 1898 "International Competition for the [Phoebe Hearst](/wiki/Phoebe_Hearst "Phoebe Hearst") Architectural Plan for the University of California", funded by [William Randolph Hearst](/wiki/William_Randolph_Hearst "William Randolph Hearst")'s mother and initially held in the Belgian city of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"); eleven finalists were judged again in San Francisco in 1899.[[175]](#cite_note-177) The winner was Frenchman [Émile Bénard](/wiki/%C3%89mile_B%C3%A9nard "Émile Bénard"), who refused to personally supervise the implementation of his plan and the task was subsequently given to architecture professor [John Galen Howard](/wiki/John_Galen_Howard "John Galen Howard"). Howard designed over twenty buildings, which set the tone for the campus up until its expansion in the 1950s and 1960s.

The structures forming the "classical core" of the campus were built in the [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") Classical style, and include [Hearst Greek Theatre](/wiki/Hearst_Greek_Theatre "Hearst Greek Theatre"), [Hearst Memorial Mining Building](/wiki/Hearst_Memorial_Mining_Building "Hearst Memorial Mining Building"), [Doe Memorial Library](/wiki/Doe_Memorial_Library "Doe Memorial Library"), California Hall, [Wheeler Hall](/wiki/Wheeler_Hall "Wheeler Hall"), Le Conte Hall, Gilman Hall, Haviland Hall, Wellman Hall, [Sather Gate](/wiki/Sather_Gate "Sather Gate"), and the [Sather Tower](/wiki/Sather_Tower "Sather Tower") (nicknamed "the Campanile" after its architectural inspiration, [St Mark's Campanile](/wiki/St_Mark%27s_Campanile "St Mark's Campanile") in Venice), the tallest university clock tower in the United States.[[176]](#cite_note-178) Buildings he regarded as temporary and non-academic were designed in shingle or [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic_in_North_America "Collegiate Gothic in North America") styles; examples of these are North Gate Hall, Dwinelle Annex, and Stephens Hall. Many of Howard's designs are recognized [California Historical Landmarks](/wiki/California_Historical_Landmark "California Historical Landmark")[[177]](#cite_note-179) and are listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").

Built in 1873 in a [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") [Second-Empire-style](/wiki/Second_Empire_architecture "Second Empire architecture"), South Hall, designed by David Farquharson, is the oldest university building in California. It, and the [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted")-designed [Piedmont Avenue](/wiki/Piedmont_Avenue_(Berkeley) "Piedmont Avenue (Berkeley)") east of the main campus, are two of the only surviving examples of the nineteenth-century campus. Other notable architects and firms whose work can be found in the campus and surrounding area are [Bernard Maybeck](/wiki/Bernard_Maybeck "Bernard Maybeck")[[178]](#cite_note-mccoy-180) (Faculty Club); [Julia Morgan](/wiki/Julia_Morgan "Julia Morgan") (Hearst Women's Gymnasium and [Julia Morgan Hall](/wiki/Julia_Morgan_Hall "Julia Morgan Hall")); [William Wurster](/wiki/William_Wurster "William Wurster") (Stern Hall); Moore Ruble Yudell (Haas School of Business); [Tod Williams Billie Tsien Architects](/wiki/Tod_Williams_Billie_Tsien_Architects "Tod Williams Billie Tsien Architects") (C.V. Starr East Asian Library), and [Diller Scofidio + Renfro](/wiki/Diller_Scofidio_%2B_Renfro "Diller Scofidio + Renfro") (Berkeley Art Museum and Pacific Film Archive).

### Natural features

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Natural features")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Strawberry_Creek_near_Dwinelle_Hall.jpg/250px-Strawberry_Creek_near_Dwinelle_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strawberry_Creek_near_Dwinelle_Hall.jpg)

The south fork of [Strawberry Creek](/wiki/Strawberry_Creek "Strawberry Creek"), as seen between Dwinelle Hall and [Lower Sproul Plaza](/wiki/Sproul_Plaza#Lower_Sproul "Sproul Plaza")

Flowing into the main campus are two branches of [Strawberry Creek](/wiki/Strawberry_Creek "Strawberry Creek"). The south fork enters a culvert upstream of the recreational complex at the mouth of Strawberry Canyon and passes beneath [California Memorial Stadium](/wiki/California_Memorial_Stadium "California Memorial Stadium") before appearing again in Faculty Glade. It then runs through the center of the campus before disappearing underground at the west end of campus. The north fork appears just east of [University House](/wiki/University_House,_Berkeley "University House, Berkeley") and runs through the glade north of the Valley Life Sciences Building, the original site of the Campus Arboretum.

Trees in the area date from the founding of the university. The campus features numerous wooded areas, including: [Founders' Rock](/wiki/Founders%27_Rock "Founders' Rock"), Faculty Glade, Grinnell Natural Area, and the [Eucalyptus](/wiki/Eucalyptus "Eucalyptus") Grove, which is both the tallest stand of such trees in the world and the tallest stand of hardwood trees in North America.[[179]](#cite_note-181) The campus sits on the [Hayward Fault](/wiki/Hayward_Fault "Hayward Fault"), which runs directly through California Memorial Stadium.[[180]](#cite_note-182)

## Student life and traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Student life and traditions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Cal_Football_From_Tightwad_Hill_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg/250px-Cal_Football_From_Tightwad_Hill_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cal_Football_From_Tightwad_Hill_-_Flickr_-_Joe_Parks.jpg)

Fans atop [Tightwad Hill](/wiki/Tightwad_Hill "Tightwad Hill") watch the [Cal Band](/wiki/Cal_Band "Cal Band"), with views of the [stadium](/wiki/California_Memorial_Stadium "California Memorial Stadium") and the [San Francisco Bay](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay "San Francisco Bay").

The official university mascot is [Oski the Bear](/wiki/Oski_(mascot) "Oski (mascot)"), who debuted in 1941. Previously, live bear cubs were used as mascots at Memorial Stadium until it was decided in 1940 that a costumed mascot would be a better alternative. Named after the [Oski-wow-wow](/wiki/The_Oski_Yell "The Oski Yell") yell, he is cared for by the Oski Committee, whose members have exclusive knowledge of the identity of the costume-wearer.[[181]](#cite_note-183) The [University of California Marching Band](/wiki/University_of_California_Marching_Band "University of California Marching Band"), which has served the university since 1891, performs at every home football game and at select road games as well. A smaller subset of the Cal Band, the Straw Hat Band, performs at basketball games, volleyball games, and other campus and community events.[[182]](#cite_note-184)

The UC Rally Committee, formed in 1901, is the official guardian of California's Spirit and Traditions. Wearing their traditional blue and gold rugbies, Rally Committee members can be seen at all major sporting and spirit events. Committee members are charged with the maintenance of the six Cal flags, the large California banner overhanging the Memorial Stadium Student Section and [Haas Pavilion](/wiki/Haas_Pavilion "Haas Pavilion"), the California Victory Cannon, Card Stunts and [The Big "C"](/wiki/The_Big_%22C%22 "The Big \"C\"") among other duties. The Rally Committee is also responsible for safekeeping of the [Stanford Axe](/wiki/Stanford_Axe "Stanford Axe") when it is in Cal's possession.[[183]](#cite_note-185)

Overlooking the main Berkeley campus from the foothills in the east, The Big "C" is an important symbol of California school spirit. The Big "C" has its roots in an early 20th-century campus event called "Rush", which pitted the freshman and sophomore classes against each other in a race up Charter Hill that often developed into a wrestling match. It was eventually decided to discontinue Rush and, in 1905, the freshman and sophomore classes banded together in a show of unity to build "the Big C."[[184]](#cite_note-186)

Students invented the college football tradition of [card stunts](/wiki/Card_stunt "Card stunt"). Then known as Bleacher Stunts, they were first performed during the 1910 [Big Game](/wiki/Big_Game_(football) "Big Game (football)") and consisted of two stunts: a picture of the Stanford Axe and a large blue "C" on a white background. The tradition is continued today by the Rally Committee in the Cal [student section](/wiki/Student_section "Student section") and incorporates complicated motions, for example tracing the Cal script logo on a blue background with an imaginary yellow pen.[[185]](#cite_note-187)

The California Victory Cannon, placed on [Tightwad Hill](/wiki/Tightwad_Hill "Tightwad Hill") overlooking the stadium, is fired before every football home game, after every score, and after every Cal victory. First used in the 1963 Big Game, it was originally placed on the sidelines before moving to Tightwad Hill in 1971. The only time the cannon ran out of ammunition was during a game against [Pacific](/wiki/University_of_the_Pacific_(United_States) "University of the Pacific (United States)") in 1991, when Cal scored 12 touchdowns.[[186]](#cite_note-188) The Cal Mic Men, a standard at home football games, has recently expanded to involve basketball and volleyball. The traditional role comes from students holding megaphones and yelling, but now includes microphones, a dedicated platform during games, and the direction of the entire student section.[[187]](#cite_note-189)

### Student housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Student housing")]

Main article: [Housing at the University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/Housing_at_the_University_of_California,_Berkeley "Housing at the University of California, Berkeley")

Berkeley students are offered a variety of housing options, including university-owned or affiliated residences, private residences, fraternities and sororities, and cooperative housing (co-ops). Berkeley students, and those of other local schools, have the option of living in one of the twenty cooperative houses participating in the Berkeley Student Cooperative (BSC), a [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit "Nonprofit") [housing cooperative](/wiki/Housing_cooperative "Housing cooperative") network consisting of 20 residences and 1250 member-owners.[[188]](#cite_note-190)

#### Fraternities and sororities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Fraternities and sororities")]

About three percent of undergraduate men and nine percent of undergraduate women—or 3,400 of total undergraduates—are active in Berkeley's Greek system.[[189]](#cite_note-191) University-sanctioned fraternities and sororities comprise over 60 houses affiliated with four Greek councils.[[190]](#cite_note-192)[[191]](#cite_note-193)

### Student-run organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Student-run organizations")]

#### Associated Students of the University of California (ASUC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Associated Students of the University of California (ASUC)")]

Main article: [Associated Students of the University of California](/wiki/Associated_Students_of_the_University_of_California "Associated Students of the University of California")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Wellness_Room.jpg/250px-Wellness_Room.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wellness_Room.jpg)

Wellness Room sleep pods: part of a program created by the ASUC, UC Berkeley's official student association.

The [Associated Students of the University of California](/wiki/Associated_Students_of_the_University_of_California "Associated Students of the University of California") (ASUC) is the official [student association](/wiki/Student_association "Student association") that controls funding for student groups and organizes on-campus student events. The two main political parties are "Student Action"[[192]](#cite_note-194) and "CalSERVE".[[193]](#cite_note-195) The organization was founded in 1887 and has an annual operating budget of $1.7 million (excluding the budget of the Graduate Assembly of the ASUC), in addition to various investment assets. Its alumni include multiple State Senators, Assemblymembers, and White House Administration officials.[[194]](#cite_note-196)

#### Media and publications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Media and publications")]

Berkeley's student-run online television station, [CalTV](/wiki/CalTV "CalTV"), was formed in 2005 and broadcasts online. It is run by students with a variety of backgrounds and majors. Since the mid-2010s, it has been a program of the [ASUC](/wiki/Associated_Students_of_the_University_of_California "Associated Students of the University of California").[[195]](#cite_note-197) Berkeley's independent student-run newspaper is *[The Daily Californian](/wiki/The_Daily_Californian "The Daily Californian")*. Founded in 1871, *The Daily Cal* became independent in 1971 after the campus administration fired three senior editors for encouraging readers to take back [People's Park](/wiki/People%27s_Park_(Berkeley) "People's Park (Berkeley)"). The Daily Californian has both a print and online edition. Berkeley's FM [student radio station](/wiki/Student_radio_station "Student radio station"), [KALX](/wiki/KALX "KALX"), broadcasts on 90.7 MHz. It is run largely by volunteers, including both students and community members. Berkeley also features an assortment of student-run publications:

* *[California Law Review](/wiki/California_Law_Review "California Law Review")*, [law journal](/wiki/Law_journal "Law journal") published by [Berkeley Law](/wiki/UC_Berkeley_School_of_Law "UC Berkeley School of Law"), est. 1912.
* *[Berkeley Poetry Review](/wiki/Berkeley_Poetry_Review "Berkeley Poetry Review")*, national [poetry journal](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry"), est. 1974.
* *[Berkeley Fiction Review](/wiki/Berkeley_Fiction_Review "Berkeley Fiction Review")*, American [literary magazine](/wiki/Literary_magazine "Literary magazine"), est. 1981.
* *[Heuristic Squelch](/wiki/Heuristic_Squelch "Heuristic Squelch")*, [satirical newspaper](/wiki/News_satire "News satire"), est. 1991.
* *[California Patriot](/wiki/California_Patriot "California Patriot")*, [conservative](/wiki/Conservative "Conservative") political magazine, est. 2000.
* *[Berkeley Political Review](/wiki/Berkeley_Political_Review "Berkeley Political Review")*, [nonpartisan](/wiki/Nonpartisanism "Nonpartisanism") political magazine, est. 2001.
* *Caliber Magazine*, an "everything magazine", featuring articles and blogs on a wide range of topics, est. 2008.
* *B-Side*, music magazine, est. 2013.
* *Smart Ass*, [liberal](/wiki/College_Democrats_of_America "College Democrats of America") magazine, est. 2015.
* *Berkeley Economic Review*, [economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics") journal, est. 2016.
* *Business Berkeley*, [Haas](/wiki/Haas_School_of_Business "Haas School of Business") undergraduate journal.

#### Student groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Student groups")]

"DeCal" redirects here. For other uses, see [DeCal (disambiguation)](/wiki/DeCal_(disambiguation) "DeCal (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Berkeley_DM_2009.JPG/250px-Berkeley_DM_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Berkeley_DM_2009.JPG)

Berkeley Dance [Marathon](/wiki/Marathon "Marathon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Zellerbach01.jpg/250px-Zellerbach01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zellerbach01.jpg)

Zellerbach Hall, home of the Cal Performances theater group

There are ninety-four political student groups on campus, including MEChXA de UC Berkeley, Berkeley [ACLU](/wiki/American_Civil_Liberties_Union "American Civil Liberties Union"), Berkeley Students for Life, Campus Greens, The Sustainability Team (STEAM), the [Berkeley Student Food Collective](/wiki/Berkeley_Student_Food_Collective "Berkeley Student Food Collective"), Students for Sensible Drug Policy, Cal Berkeley Democrats, and the Berkeley College Republicans.[[196]](#cite_note-198) The Residence Hall Assembly (RHA) is the student-led umbrella organization that oversees event planning, legislation, sponsorships and other activities for over 7,200 on-campus undergraduate residents.[[197]](#cite_note-199)

Berkeley students also run a number of consulting groups, including the Berkeley Group, founded in 2003 and affiliated with the Haas School.[[198]](#cite_note-200) Students from various concentrations are recruited and trained to work on pro-bono consulting engagements with actual nonprofit clients. Berkeley Consulting, founded in 1996, has served over 140 companies across the high-tech, retail, banking, and non-profit sectors.[[199]](#cite_note-201)

ImagiCal has been the college chapter of the [American Advertising Federation](/wiki/American_Advertising_Federation "American Advertising Federation") at Berkeley since the late 1980s.[[200]](#cite_note-202) The team competes annually in the National Student Advertising Competition, with students from disparate majors working together on a marketing case underwritten by a corporate sponsor. The [Berkeley Forum](/wiki/Berkeley_Forum "Berkeley Forum") is a nonpartisan student organization that hosts panels, debates, and speeches across a variety of fields.[[201]](#cite_note-203) Past speakers include [Senator](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") [Rand Paul](/wiki/Rand_Paul "Rand Paul"), entrepreneur and venture capitalist [Peter Thiel](/wiki/Peter_Thiel "Peter Thiel"), and [Khan Academy](/wiki/Khan_Academy "Khan Academy") founder [Salman Khan](/wiki/Salman_Khan_(educator) "Salman Khan (educator)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/UCSO.jpg/250px-UCSO.jpg)](/wiki/File:UCSO.jpg)

UC Berkeley Symphony Orchestra

Democratic Education at Cal, or DeCal, is a program that promotes the creation of professor-sponsored, student-facilitated classes.[[202]](#cite_note-204) DeCal arose out of the 1960s [Free Speech movement](/wiki/Free_Speech_movement "Free Speech movement") and was officially established in 1981. The program offers around 150 courses on a vast range of subjects that appeal to the student community, including classes on the [Rubik's Cube](/wiki/Rubik%27s_Cube "Rubik's Cube"), [blockchain](/wiki/Blockchain "Blockchain"), [web design](/wiki/Web_design "Web design"), metamodernism, [cooking](/wiki/Cooking "Cooking"), Jewish art, [3D animation](/wiki/3D_animation "3D animation"), and [bioprinting](/wiki/3D_bioprinting "3D bioprinting").[[203]](#cite_note-205)

The campus is home to several [a cappella](/wiki/A_cappella "A cappella") groups, including Drawn to Scale, Artists in Resonance, Berkeley Dil Se, the [UC Men's Octet](/wiki/UC_Men%27s_Octet "UC Men's Octet"), the [California Golden Overtones](/wiki/California_Golden_Overtones "California Golden Overtones"), DeCadence, and Noteworthy. The [University of California Men's Octet](/wiki/University_of_California_Men%27s_Octet "University of California Men's Octet") was founded in 1948. Since 1967, students and staff jazz musicians have had an opportunity to perform and study with the [University of California Jazz Ensembles](/wiki/University_of_California_Jazz_Ensembles "University of California Jazz Ensembles"). For several decades it hosted the Pacific Coast Collegiate Jazz Festival, part of the American Collegiate Jazz Festival, a competitive forum for student musicians. PCCJF brought jazz artists including [Hubert Laws](/wiki/Hubert_Laws "Hubert Laws"), [Sonny Rollins](/wiki/Sonny_Rollins "Sonny Rollins"), [Freddie Hubbard](/wiki/Freddie_Hubbard "Freddie Hubbard"), and [Ed Shaughnessy](/wiki/Ed_Shaughnessy "Ed Shaughnessy") to the Berkeley campus as performers. Berkeley also hosts other performing arts groups in comedy, dance, acting and instrumental music.

#### Engineering student teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Engineering student teams")]

Given Berkeley's [STEM](/wiki/Science,_technology,_engineering,_and_mathematics "Science, technology, engineering, and mathematics") education, there are a variety of student-run engineering teams that focus on winning design and engineering competitions.
Berkeley has two prominent [amateur rocketry](/wiki/Amateur_rocketry "Amateur rocketry") teams: Space Enterprise at Berkeley (SEB)[[204]](#cite_note-206) and Space Technologies and Rocketry (STAR).[[205]](#cite_note-207) Both have launched solid-fuel [sounding rockets](/wiki/Sounding_rocket "Sounding rocket") and are currently developing [liquid propellant rockets](/wiki/Liquid-propellant_rocket "Liquid-propellant rocket"). The university also has two [Formula SAE](/wiki/Formula_SAE "Formula SAE") teams: Berkeley Formula Racing[[206]](#cite_note-208) and Formula Electric Berkeley.[[207]](#cite_note-209) Both of these teams participate in Formula SAE–run competitions, with the former focusing on internal combustion engines and the latter on electric motors. Berkeley has a number of other vehicle teams, including CalSol,[[208]](#cite_note-210) CalSMV,[[209]](#cite_note-211) and Human Powered Vehicle.[[210]](#cite_note-212)

### Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Athletics")]

Main article: [California Golden Bears](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears "California Golden Bears")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/California_Memorial_Stadium_2015.jpg/250px-California_Memorial_Stadium_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:California_Memorial_Stadium_2015.jpg)

The base of the [California Memorial Stadium](/wiki/California_Memorial_Stadium "California Memorial Stadium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Haas_Pavilion_Court.jpg/250px-Haas_Pavilion_Court.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haas_Pavilion_Court.jpg)

The interior of [Haas Pavilion](/wiki/Haas_Pavilion "Haas Pavilion") during a [Cal Basketball](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears_men%27s_basketball "California Golden Bears men's basketball") game

The university's athletic teams are known as the [California Golden Bears](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears "California Golden Bears"), often shortened to "Cal Bears" or just "Cal", and were historically members of the NCAA Division I [Pac-12 Conference](/wiki/Pac-12_Conference "Pac-12 Conference") (Pac-12). In 2024, Cal joined the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") (ACC).[[211]](#cite_note-213) Cal is also a member of the [Mountain Pacific Sports Federation](/wiki/Mountain_Pacific_Sports_Federation "Mountain Pacific Sports Federation") in several sports not sponsored by the ACC. The first school colors, established in 1873 by a committee of students, were [Yale Blue](/wiki/Yale_Blue "Yale Blue") and gold.[[212]](#cite_note-colors-214)[[213]](#cite_note-Resource_Guide:_Student_history-215) Yale Blue was originally chosen because many of the university's inaugural faculty were Yale graduates, including Henry Durant, its first president. Blue and gold were specified and made the official colors of the university and the state colors of California in 1955.[[212]](#cite_note-colors-214)[[214]](#cite_note-216) In 2014, the athletic department specified a darker blue.[[215]](#cite_note-AthleticColors-217)[[216]](#cite_note-BrandColors-218)

The [California Golden Bears](/wiki/California_Golden_Bears "California Golden Bears") have won national championships in baseball (2), men's basketball (2), men's crew (15), women's crew (3), football (5), men's golf (1), men's gymnastics (4), men's lacrosse (1), men's rugby (26), softball (1), men's swimming & diving (4), women's swimming & diving (3), men's tennis (1), men's track & field (1), and men's water polo (13). Students and alumni have also won [207 Olympic medals](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medals "List of American universities with Olympic medals").[[217]](#cite_note-219)

California finished in first place in the 2007–08 Fall U.S. Sports Academy Directors' Cup standings (now the [NACDA Directors' Cup](/wiki/NACDA_Directors%27_Cup "NACDA Directors' Cup")), a competition measuring the best overall collegiate athletic programs in the country, with points awarded for national finishes in NCAA sports.[[218]](#cite_note-220) It finished the 2007–08 competition in seventh place with 1119 points.[[219]](#cite_note-221)
Most recently, California finished in third place in the 2010–11 NACDA Directors' Cup with 1219.50 points, finishing behind Stanford and Ohio State. This is California's highest ever finish in the Director's Cup.[[220]](#cite_note-222) The Golden Bears' traditional arch-rival is the [Stanford Cardinal](/wiki/Stanford_Cardinal "Stanford Cardinal"), and the most anticipated sporting event between the two universities is the annual football match dubbed the [Big Game](/wiki/Big_Game_(American_football) "Big Game (American football)"), celebrated with spirit events on both campuses. Since 1933, the winner of the Big Game has been awarded custody of [the Stanford Axe](/wiki/The_Stanford_Axe "The Stanford Axe"). Other sporting games between these rivals have related names such as the Big Splash (water polo) or the Big Kick (soccer).[[221]](#cite_note-223)

## Notable alumni, faculty, and staff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Notable alumni, faculty, and staff")]

### Faculty and staff

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Faculty and staff")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of California, Berkeley faculty](/wiki/List_of_University_of_California,_Berkeley_faculty "List of University of California, Berkeley faculty").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/University_of_California_Radiation_Laboratory_staff_on_the_magnet_yoke_for_the_60-inch_cyclotron%2C_1938.jpg/250px-University_of_California_Radiation_Laboratory_staff_on_the_magnet_yoke_for_the_60-inch_cyclotron%2C_1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_California_Radiation_Laboratory_staff_on_the_magnet_yoke_for_the_60-inch_cyclotron,_1938.jpg)

University of California Radiation Laboratory staff on the magnet yoke for the 60-inch cyclotron, 1938; Nobel prize-winners [Ernest Lawrence](/wiki/Ernest_Lawrence "Ernest Lawrence"), [Edwin McMillan](/wiki/Edwin_McMillan "Edwin McMillan"), and [Luis Alvarez](/wiki/Luis_Walter_Alvarez "Luis Walter Alvarez") are shown, in addition to [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer") and [Robert R. Wilson](/wiki/Robert_R._Wilson "Robert R. Wilson").

* [Shiing-Shen Chern](/wiki/Shiing-Shen_Chern "Shiing-Shen Chern"), a leading geometer of the 20th century, co-founded the [Mathematical Sciences Research Institute](/wiki/Mathematical_Sciences_Research_Institute "Mathematical Sciences Research Institute") and served as its founding director until 1984.[[222]](#cite_note-224)[[52]](#cite_note-MSRI_history-52)
* Physicist [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer") was scientific director of the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project") and was the founder of the Berkeley Center for Theoretical Physics.[[223]](#cite_note-225)
* Faculty member [Edward Teller](/wiki/Edward_Teller "Edward Teller") was (together with [Stanislaw Ulam](/wiki/Stanislaw_Ulam "Stanislaw Ulam")) the "father of the [hydrogen bomb](/wiki/Thermonuclear_weapon "Thermonuclear weapon")", who laid important foundations for the establishment of [Space Sciences Laboratory](/wiki/Space_Sciences_Laboratory "Space Sciences Laboratory") at Berkeley.[[224]](#cite_note-226)
* [Ernest Lawrence](/wiki/Ernest_Lawrence "Ernest Lawrence"), a Nobel laureate in physics who invented the [cyclotron](/wiki/Cyclotron "Cyclotron") at Berkeley, founded the Radiation Laboratory on campus, which later became the [Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory](/wiki/Lawrence_Berkeley_National_Laboratory "Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory").[[225]](#cite_note-227)
* [Gilbert N. Lewis](/wiki/Gilbert_N._Lewis "Gilbert N. Lewis"), former dean of the College of Chemistry, was nominated 41 times for the [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry").[[226]](#cite_note-NobelPrize-228)[[227]](#cite_note-229) He mentored and influenced numerous Berkeley Nobel laureates, including [Harold Urey](/wiki/Harold_Urey "Harold Urey") (1934 Nobel Prize), [William F. Giauque](/wiki/William_F._Giauque "William F. Giauque") (1949 Nobel Prize), [Glenn T. Seaborg](/wiki/Glenn_T._Seaborg "Glenn T. Seaborg") (1951 Nobel Prize), [Willard Libby](/wiki/Willard_Libby "Willard Libby") (1960 Nobel Prize), and [Melvin Calvin](/wiki/Melvin_Calvin "Melvin Calvin") (1961 Nobel Prize).[[228]](#cite_note-Lemelson-MIT-230)[[229]](#cite_note-231)
* [Glenn T. Seaborg](/wiki/Glenn_T._Seaborg "Glenn T. Seaborg"), a Nobel laureate in chemistry, discovered or co-discovered ten chemical elements at Berkeley and served as chancellor from 1958 to 1961.[[230]](#cite_note-232)[[231]](#cite_note-233)
* [Hans Albert Einstein](/wiki/Hans_Albert_Einstein "Hans Albert Einstein"), the first son of [Albert Einstein](/wiki/Albert_Einstein "Albert Einstein") and a world's leading scholar in [hydraulic engineering](/wiki/Hydraulic_engineering "Hydraulic engineering"), was a long-time faculty member at Berkeley.[[232]](#cite_note-234)
* [Steven Chu](/wiki/Steven_Chu "Steven Chu") (PhD 1976), the 12th [United States Secretary of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Energy "United States Secretary of Energy") and Nobel laureate in physics, was director of [Berkeley Lab](/wiki/Berkeley_Lab "Berkeley Lab") from 2004 to 2009.
* [Janet Yellen](/wiki/Janet_Yellen "Janet Yellen"), 78th [United States Secretary of Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_Treasury "United States Secretary of Treasury") and the 15th [chair of the Federal Reserve](/wiki/Chair_of_the_Federal_Reserve "Chair of the Federal Reserve"), is a professor emeritus at Berkeley [Haas School of Business](/wiki/Haas_School_of_Business "Haas School of Business") and the Department of Economics.[[233]](#cite_note-235)[[234]](#cite_note-236)

### Alumni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Alumni")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of California, Berkeley alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_California,_Berkeley_alumni "List of University of California, Berkeley alumni").

Alumni have included 260 [American Academy of Arts and Sciences](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Sciences "American Academy of Arts and Sciences") Fellows,[[235]](#cite_note-237) 34 [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") winners, 25 [living billionaire alumni](/wiki/List_of_universities_by_number_of_billionaire_alumni "List of universities by number of billionaire alumni"),[[236]](#cite_note-238) 22 [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(government) "Cabinet (government)") members, 68 recipients of the [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science"), 190 recipients of the [MacArthur Fellowship](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellowship "MacArthur Fellowship"),[[237]](#cite_note-239) 144 members of the [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/United_States_National_Academy_of_Sciences "United States National Academy of Sciences"),[[238]](#cite_note-240) 139 [Guggenheim Fellows](/wiki/Guggenheim_Fellows "Guggenheim Fellows"), and 125 [Sloan Fellows](/wiki/Sloan_Fellows "Sloan Fellows"), and 75 members of the [National Academy of Engineering](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Engineering "National Academy of Engineering").[[239]](#cite_note-241)[[240]](#cite_note-242)

Berkeley alumni have served in a range of prominent government offices, both domestic and foreign, including [Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States_Supreme_Court "Chief Justice of the United States Supreme Court") ([Earl Warren](/wiki/Earl_Warren "Earl Warren"), BA, JD); [United States Attorney General](/wiki/United_States_Attorney_General "United States Attorney General") ([Edwin Meese](/wiki/Edwin_Meese "Edwin Meese") III, JD); [United States Secretary of State](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_State "United States Secretary of State") ([Dean Rusk](/wiki/Dean_Rusk "Dean Rusk"), LLB); [United States Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury") ([W. O'Brien"), JD); scores of federal judges and members of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") ([10 currently serving](/wiki/List_of_current_United_States_representatives "List of current United States representatives")) and [United States Foreign Service](/wiki/United_States_Foreign_Service "United States Foreign Service"); governors of California ([George C. Pardee](/wiki/George_C._Pardee "George C. Pardee"); [Hiram W. Johnson](/wiki/Hiram_W._Johnson "Hiram W. Johnson"); [Earl Warren](/wiki/Earl_Warren "Earl Warren"), BA and LLB; [Jerry Brown](/wiki/Jerry_Brown "Jerry Brown"), BA; and [Pete Wilson](/wiki/Pete_Wilson "Pete Wilson"), JD), Michigan ([Jennifer Granholm](/wiki/Jennifer_Granholm "Jennifer Granholm"), BA), and the United States Virgin Islands ([Walter A. Gordon](/wiki/Walter_A._Gordon "Walter A. Gordon"), BA); Lieutenant General of the United States Army ([Jimmy Doolittle](/wiki/Jimmy_Doolittle "Jimmy Doolittle"), BA); Major General of the United States Marine Corps ([Oliver Prince Smith](/wiki/Oliver_Prince_Smith "Oliver Prince Smith")); Brigadier General of the United States Marine Corps ([Bertram A. Bone](/wiki/Bertram_A._Bone "Bertram A. Bone"), BS); [Director of the Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Director_of_the_Central_Intelligence_Agency "Director of the Central Intelligence Agency") and Chairman of the Atomic Energy Commission ([John A. McCone](/wiki/John_A._McCone "John A. McCone"), BS); chair and members of the [Council of Economic Advisers](/wiki/Council_of_Economic_Advisers "Council of Economic Advisers") ([Michael Boskin](/wiki/Michael_Boskin "Michael Boskin"), BA, PhD.; Sandra Black, BA; Jesse Rothstein, PhD; Robert Seamans, PhD; Jay Shambaugh, PhD; James Stock, MA, PhD); Governor of the Federal Reserve System ([H. Robert Heller](/wiki/H._Robert_Heller "H. Robert Heller"), PhD) and President and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York ([William C. Dudley](/wiki/William_C._Dudley "William C. Dudley"), PhD); Commissioners of the [SEC](/wiki/Securities_and_Exchange_Commission "Securities and Exchange Commission") ([Troy A. Paredes](/wiki/Troy_A._Paredes "Troy A. Paredes"), BA) and the [FCC](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (Rachelle Chong, BA); and [United States Surgeon General](/wiki/United_States_Surgeon_General "United States Surgeon General") ([Kenneth P. Moritsugu](/wiki/Kenneth_P._Moritsugu "Kenneth P. Moritsugu"), MPH).

Foreign alumni include the [president of Colombia](/wiki/President_of_Colombia "President of Colombia") 1922–1926 ([Pedro Nel Ospina Vázquez](/wiki/Pedro_Nel_Ospina_V%C3%A1zquez "Pedro Nel Ospina Vázquez"), BA); the [president of Mexico](/wiki/President_of_Mexico "President of Mexico") ([Francisco I. Madero](/wiki/Francisco_I._Madero "Francisco I. Madero"), attended 1892–93); the president and prime minister of Pakistan ([Zulfikar Ali Bhutto](/wiki/Zulfikar_Ali_Bhutto "Zulfikar Ali Bhutto")); the premier of the Republic of China ([Sun Fo](/wiki/Sun_Fo "Sun Fo"), BA); the president of Costa Rica (Miguel Angel Rodriguez, MA, PhD); and members of parliament of the United Kingdom ([House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"), [Lydia Dunn, Baroness Dunn](/wiki/Lydia_Dunn,_Baroness_Dunn "Lydia Dunn, Baroness Dunn"), BS), India ([Rajya Sabha](/wiki/Rajya_Sabha "Rajya Sabha"), the upper house, [Prithviraj Chavan](/wiki/Prithviraj_Chavan "Prithviraj Chavan"), MS); Iran ([Mohammad Javad Larijani](/wiki/Mohammad_Javad_Larijani "Mohammad Javad Larijani"), PhD); Nigerian Minister of Science and Technology and first executive governor of Abia State ([Ogbonnaya Onu](/wiki/Ogbonnaya_Onu "Ogbonnaya Onu"), PhD); Barbados' ambassador to Brazil ([Tonika Sealy-Thompson](/wiki/Tonika_Sealy-Thompson "Tonika Sealy-Thompson"), PhD). Alumni have also served in many supranational posts, notable among which are president of the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank_Group "World Bank Group") ([Robert McNamara](/wiki/Robert_McNamara "Robert McNamara"), BS); Deputy Prime Minister of Spain and managing director of the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") ([Rodrigo Rato](/wiki/Rodrigo_Rato "Rodrigo Rato"), MBA); executive director of [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF") ([Ann Veneman](/wiki/Ann_Veneman "Ann Veneman"), MPP); member of the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") ([Bruno Megret](/wiki/Bruno_Megret "Bruno Megret"), MS); and judge of the [World Court](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") ([Joan Donoghue](/wiki/Joan_Donoghue "Joan Donoghue"), JD).

#### Science

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Science")]

[![Harold Urey, PhD 1923, Nobel laureate and discoverer of deuterium](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Harold_Urey%2C_1934.jpg/250px-Harold_Urey%2C_1934.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harold_Urey,_1934.jpg)

[Harold Urey](/wiki/Harold_Urey "Harold Urey"), PhD 1923, [Nobel](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") laureate and discoverer of [deuterium](/wiki/Deuterium "Deuterium")

[Nobel](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize") laureate [William F. Giauque](/wiki/William_F._Giauque "William F. Giauque") (BS 1920, PhD 1922) investigated [chemical thermodynamics](/wiki/Chemical_thermodynamics "Chemical thermodynamics"), Nobel laureate [Willard Libby](/wiki/Willard_Libby "Willard Libby") (BS 1931, PhD 1933) pioneered [radiocarbon dating](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating"), Nobel laureate [Willis Lamb](/wiki/Willis_Lamb "Willis Lamb") (BS 1934, PhD 1938) examined the [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") [spectrum](/wiki/Spectrum "Spectrum"), Nobel laureate [Hamilton O. Smith](/wiki/Hamilton_O._Smith "Hamilton O. Smith") (BA 1952) applied [restriction enzymes](/wiki/Restriction_enzymes "Restriction enzymes") to [molecular genetics](/wiki/Molecular_genetics "Molecular genetics"), Nobel laureate [Robert Laughlin](/wiki/Robert_Laughlin "Robert Laughlin") (BA 1972) explored the [fractional quantum Hall effect](/wiki/Fractional_quantum_Hall_effect "Fractional quantum Hall effect"), and Nobel laureate [Andrew Fire](/wiki/Andrew_Fire "Andrew Fire") (BA 1978) helped to discover [RNA interference](/wiki/RNA_interference "RNA interference")-[gene silencing](/wiki/Gene_silencing "Gene silencing") by double-stranded [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA"). Nobel laureate [Glenn T. Seaborg](/wiki/Glenn_T._Seaborg "Glenn T. Seaborg") (PhD 1937) collaborated with [Albert Ghiorso](/wiki/Albert_Ghiorso "Albert Ghiorso") (BS 1913) to discover twelve chemical elements, such as *[americium](/wiki/Americium "Americium")*, *[berkelium](/wiki/Berkelium "Berkelium")*, and *[californium](/wiki/Californium "Californium")*. [David Bohm](/wiki/David_Bohm "David Bohm") (PhD 1943) discovered [Bohm diffusion](/wiki/Bohm_diffusion "Bohm diffusion"). Nobel laureate [Yuan T. Lee](/wiki/Yuan_T._Lee "Yuan T. Lee") (PhD 1965) developed the [crossed molecular beam](/wiki/Crossed_molecular_beam "Crossed molecular beam") technique for studying chemical reactions. [Carol Greider](/wiki/Carol_Greider "Carol Greider") (PhD 1987) was awarded the 2009 [Nobel Prize in medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_medicine "Nobel Prize in medicine") for discovering a key mechanism in the genetic operations of cells. [Harvey Itano](/wiki/Harvey_Itano "Harvey Itano") (BS 1942) conducted breakthrough work on [sickle cell anemia](/wiki/Sickle_cell_anemia "Sickle cell anemia") that marked the first time a disease was linked to a molecular origin.[[241]](#cite_note-maugh-243)

[Narendra Karmarkar](/wiki/Narendra_Karmarkar "Narendra Karmarkar") (PhD 1983) is known for the interior point method, a polynomial algorithm for linear programming known as [Karmarkar's algorithm](/wiki/Karmarkar%27s_algorithm "Karmarkar's algorithm").[[242]](#cite_note-mg-244) [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science") laureate [Chien-Shiung Wu](/wiki/Chien-Shiung_Wu "Chien-Shiung Wu") (PhD 1940), often known as the "Chinese Madame Curie", disproved the Law of Conservation of [Parity](/wiki/Parity_(physics) "Parity (physics)") for which she was awarded the inaugural [Wolf Prize in Physics](/wiki/Wolf_Prize_in_Physics "Wolf Prize in Physics").[[243]](#cite_note-245) [Kary Mullis](/wiki/Kary_Mullis "Kary Mullis") (PhD 1973) was awarded the 1993 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") for his role in developing the [polymerase chain reaction](/wiki/Polymerase_chain_reaction "Polymerase chain reaction"),[[244]](#cite_note-246) a method for amplifying [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") sequences. [Christine Essenberg](/wiki/Christine_Essenberg "Christine Essenberg") was one of the first women to earn a PhD in zoology (MA1914, PhD 1917) known for her expertise in [polychaetes](/wiki/Polychaete "Polychaete") and [plankton](/wiki/Plankton "Plankton") studies. [Olga Hartman](/wiki/Olga_Hartman "Olga Hartman") (MA 1933, PhD 1936) was a zoologist who described hundreds of species of [polychaete worms](/wiki/Polychaete_worms "Polychaete worms").[[245]](#cite_note-247)[[246]](#cite_note-248)[[247]](#cite_note-249) [Edward P. Tryon](/wiki/Edward_P._Tryon "Edward P. Tryon") (PhD 1967) is the physicist who first said our universe originated from a quantum fluctuation of the vacuum.[[248]](#cite_note-250)[[249]](#cite_note-251)[[250]](#cite_note-252) [John N. Bahcall](/wiki/John_N._Bahcall "John N. Bahcall") (BS 1956) worked on the [Standard Solar Model](/wiki/Standard_Solar_Model "Standard Solar Model") and the [Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope"),[[251]](#cite_note-Times2005_09_01-253) resulting in a [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science").[[251]](#cite_note-Times2005_09_01-253) [Peter Smith](/wiki/Peter_Smith_(physicist) "Peter Smith (physicist)") (BS 1969) was the [principal investigator](/wiki/Principal_investigator "Principal investigator") and project leader for the [NASA](/wiki/NASA "NASA") robotic explorer *[Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix_(spacecraft) "Phoenix (spacecraft)")*,[[252]](#cite_note-254) which physically confirmed the presence of water on the planet [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") for the first time.[[253]](#cite_note-255) Astronauts [James van Hoften](/wiki/James_van_Hoften "James van Hoften") (BS 1966), [Margaret Rhea Seddon](/wiki/Margaret_Rhea_Seddon "Margaret Rhea Seddon") (BA 1970), [Leroy Chiao](/wiki/Leroy_Chiao "Leroy Chiao") (BS 1983), and [Rex Walheim](/wiki/Rex_Walheim "Rex Walheim") (BS 1984) have orbited the Earth in NASA's fleet of [Space Shuttles](/wiki/Space_Shuttle "Space Shuttle").

#### Computers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Computers")]

Berkeley alumni have developed a number of key technologies associated with the [personal computer](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") and the Internet.[[254]](#cite_note-256) [Unix](/wiki/Unix "Unix") was created by alumnus [Ken Thompson](/wiki/Ken_Thompson "Ken Thompson") (BS 1965, MS 1966) along with colleague [Dennis Ritchie](/wiki/Dennis_Ritchie "Dennis Ritchie"). Alumni such as [L. Peter Deutsch](/wiki/L._Peter_Deutsch "L. Peter Deutsch")[[255]](#cite_note-257)[[256]](#cite_note-258)[[257]](#cite_note-259) (PhD 1973), [Butler Lampson](/wiki/Butler_Lampson "Butler Lampson") (PhD 1967), and [Charles P. Thacker](/wiki/Charles_P._Thacker "Charles P. Thacker") (BS 1967)[[258]](#cite_note-260) worked with Ken Thompson on [Project Genie](/wiki/Project_Genie "Project Genie") and then formed the ill-fated [US Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense")-funded Berkeley Computer Corporation (BCC), which was scattered throughout the Berkeley campus in non-descript offices to avoid anti-war protestors.[[259]](#cite_note-261) After BCC failed, Deutsch, Lampson, and Thacker joined [Xerox PARC](/wiki/Xerox_PARC "Xerox PARC"), where they developed a number of pioneering computer technologies, culminating in the [Xerox Alto](/wiki/Xerox_Alto "Xerox Alto") that inspired the [Apple Macintosh](/wiki/Apple_Macintosh "Apple Macintosh"). In particular, the Alto used a [computer mouse](/wiki/Computer_mouse "Computer mouse"), which had been invented by [Doug Engelbart](/wiki/Doug_Engelbart "Doug Engelbart") (BEng 1952, PhD 1955). Thompson, Lampson, Engelbart, and Thacker[[260]](#cite_note-262) all later received a Turing Award. Also at Xerox PARC was Ronald Schmidt (BS 1966, MS 1968, PhD 1971), who became known as "the man who brought [Ethernet](/wiki/Ethernet "Ethernet") to the masses."[[261]](#cite_note-263)

Another Xerox PARC researcher, [Charles Simonyi](/wiki/Charles_Simonyi "Charles Simonyi") (BS 1972), pioneered the first [WYSIWIG](/wiki/WYSIWIG "WYSIWIG") [word processor](/wiki/Word_processor "Word processor") program and was recruited personally by [Bill Gates](/wiki/Bill_Gates "Bill Gates") to join the fledgling company known as [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") to create [Microsoft Word](/wiki/Microsoft_Word "Microsoft Word"). Simonyi later became the first repeat [space tourist](/wiki/Space_tourist "Space tourist"), blasting off on Russian [Soyuz](/wiki/Soyuz_(rocket) "Soyuz (rocket)") rockets to work at the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") orbiting the Earth. In 1977, a graduate student in the computer science department named Bill Joy (MS 1982) assembled[[262]](#cite_note-264) the original [Berkeley Software Distribution](/wiki/Berkeley_Software_Distribution "Berkeley Software Distribution"), commonly known as [BSD Unix](/wiki/BSD_Unix "BSD Unix"). Joy, who went on to co-found Sun Microsystems, also developed the original version of the [terminal](/wiki/Computer_display "Computer display") console editor [vi](/wiki/Vi_(text_editor) "Vi (text editor)"), while [Ken Arnold](/wiki/Ken_Arnold "Ken Arnold") (BA 1985) created [Curses](/wiki/Curses_(programming_library) "Curses (programming library)"), a terminal control [library](/wiki/Library_(computer_science) "Library (computer science)") for [Unix-like](/wiki/Unix-like "Unix-like") systems that enables the construction of [text user interface (TUI)](/wiki/Text_user_interface "Text user interface") applications. Working alongside Joy at Berkeley were undergraduates [William Jolitz](/wiki/William_Jolitz "William Jolitz") (BS 1997) and his future wife [Lynne Jolitz](/wiki/Lynne_Jolitz "Lynne Jolitz") (BA 1989), who together created [386BSD](/wiki/386BSD "386BSD"), a version of BSD Unix that runs on Intel CPUs and evolved into the [BSD family of free operating systems](/wiki/Comparison_of_BSD_operating_systems "Comparison of BSD operating systems") and the [Darwin operating system](/wiki/Darwin_(operating_system) "Darwin (operating system)") underlying Apple [Mac OS X](/wiki/Mac_OS_X "Mac OS X").[[263]](#cite_note-265) [Eric Allman](/wiki/Eric_Allman "Eric Allman") (BS 1977, MS 1980) created [SendMail](/wiki/SendMail "SendMail"), a Unix [mail transfer agent](/wiki/Mail_transfer_agent "Mail transfer agent") that delivers about twelve percent of the [email](/wiki/Email "Email") in the world.[[264]](#cite_note-266)

The [XCF](/wiki/EXperimental_Computing_Facility "EXperimental Computing Facility"), an undergraduate research group located in [Soda Hall](/wiki/Soda_Hall "Soda Hall"), has been responsible for a number of notable software projects, including [GTK+](/wiki/GTK%2B "GTK+") ([Peter Mattis](/wiki/Peter_Mattis "Peter Mattis"), BS 1997), [The GIMP](/wiki/The_GIMP "The GIMP") ([Spencer Kimball](/wiki/Spencer_Kimball_(computer_programmer) "Spencer Kimball (computer programmer)"), BS 1996), and the initial diagnosis of the [Morris worm](/wiki/Morris_worm "Morris worm").[[265]](#cite_note-267) In 1992, [Pei-Yuan Wei](/wiki/Pei-Yuan_Wei "Pei-Yuan Wei") (BS 1990)[[266]](#cite_note-268) an undergraduate at the XCF, created [ViolaWWW](/wiki/ViolaWWW "ViolaWWW"), one of the first graphical web browsers. ViolaWWW was the first browser to have embedded scriptable objects, stylesheets, and tables. He donated the code to Sun Microsystems, inspiring [Java](/wiki/Java_(programming_language) "Java (programming language)") applets. ViolaWWW also inspired researchers at the [National Center for Supercomputing Applications](/wiki/National_Center_for_Supercomputing_Applications "National Center for Supercomputing Applications") to create the [Mosaic web browser](/wiki/Mosaic_web_browser "Mosaic web browser"),[[267]](#cite_note-269) a pioneering [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") that became Microsoft [Internet Explorer](/wiki/Internet_Explorer "Internet Explorer").

#### Billionaires

[Billionaire alumni](/wiki/List_of_universities_by_number_of_billionaire_alumni "List of universities by number of billionaire alumni") include [Gordon Moore](/wiki/Gordon_Moore "Gordon Moore") (Intel founder), [James Harris Simons](/wiki/James_Harris_Simons "James Harris Simons") ([Renaissance Technologies](/wiki/Renaissance_Technologies "Renaissance Technologies")), [Masayoshi Son](/wiki/Masayoshi_Son "Masayoshi Son") (SoftBank),[[268]](#cite_note-270) Jon Stryker (Stryker Medical Equipment),[[269]](#cite_note-271) [Eric Schmidt](/wiki/Eric_Schmidt "Eric Schmidt") (former Google Chairman) and [Wendy Schmidt](/wiki/Wendy_Schmidt "Wendy Schmidt"), [Michael Milken](/wiki/Michael_Milken "Michael Milken"), Bassam Alghanim, Kutayba Alghanim,[[270]](#cite_note-272) [Charles Simonyi](/wiki/Charles_Simonyi "Charles Simonyi") (Microsoft), [Cher Wang](/wiki/Cher_Wang "Cher Wang") (HTC), [Robert Haas](/wiki/Bob_Haas "Bob Haas") ([Levi Strauss & Co.](/wiki/Levi_Strauss_%26_Co. "Levi Strauss & Co.")), [Carlos Rodríguez-Pastor](/wiki/Carlos_Rodr%C3%ADguez-Pastor "Carlos Rodríguez-Pastor") (Interbank, Peru),[[271]](#cite_note-273) [Fayez Sarofim](/wiki/Fayez_Sarofim "Fayez Sarofim"), [Daniel S. Loeb](/wiki/Daniel_S._Loeb "Daniel S. Loeb"), [Paul Merage](/wiki/Paul_Merage "Paul Merage"), [David Hindawi](/wiki/David_Hindawi "David Hindawi"), [Orion Hindawi](/wiki/Orion_Hindawi "Orion Hindawi"), [Bill Joy](/wiki/Bill_Joy "Bill Joy") (Sun Microsystems founder), [Victor Koo](/wiki/Victor_Koo "Victor Koo"), [Tony Xu](/wiki/Tony_Xu "Tony Xu") (DoorDash), [Lowell Milken](/wiki/Lowell_Milken "Lowell Milken"), [Nathaniel Simons](/wiki/Nathaniel_Simons "Nathaniel Simons") and Laura Baxter-Simons, Liong Tek Kwee and Liong Seen Kwee,[[272]](#cite_note-Forbes_Kwee-274) Elizabeth Simons and Mark Heising,[[273]](#cite_note-275) [Oleg Tinkov](/wiki/Oleg_Tinkov "Oleg Tinkov"), and [Alice Schwartz](/wiki/Alice_Schwartz "Alice Schwartz").

#### Pulitzer Prize winners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Pulitzer Prize winners")]

Pulitzer Prize–winning journalist [Marguerite Higgins](/wiki/Marguerite_Higgins "Marguerite Higgins") (BA 1941) was a pioneering female war correspondent[[274]](#cite_note-276)[[275]](#cite_note-277) who covered World War II, the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), and the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War").[[276]](#cite_note-278) Novelist [Robert Penn Warren](/wiki/Robert_Penn_Warren "Robert Penn Warren") (MA 1927) won three Pulitzer Prizes,[[277]](#cite_note-279) including one for his novel *[All the King's Men](/wiki/All_the_King%27s_Men "All the King's Men")*, which was later made into an Academy Award-winning[[278]](#cite_note-280) [movie](/wiki/All_the_King%27s_Men_(1949_film) "All the King's Men (1949 film)"). Pulitzer Prize–winning cartoonist [Rube Goldberg](/wiki/Rube_Goldberg "Rube Goldberg") (BS 1904) invented the comically complex—yet ultimately trivial—contraptions known as [Rube Goldberg machines](/wiki/Rube_Goldberg_machine "Rube Goldberg machine"). Journalist Alexandra Berzon (MA 2006) won a Pulitzer Prize in 2009,[[279]](#cite_note-281) and journalist [Matt Richtel](/wiki/Matt_Richtel "Matt Richtel") (BA 1989), who also coauthors the comic strip *[Rudy Park](/wiki/Rudy_Park "Rudy Park")* under the pen name Theron Heir,[[280]](#cite_note-282) won the 2010 [Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_National_Reporting "Pulitzer Prize for National Reporting").[[281]](#cite_note-283) Pulitzer Prize–winning historian [Leon Litwack](/wiki/Leon_Litwack "Leon Litwack") (BA[[282]](#cite_note-284) 1951, PhD 1958) taught as a professor at UC Berkeley for 43 years;[[283]](#cite_note-285) [three other](/wiki/List_of_University_of_California,_Berkeley_faculty#Pulitzer_Prize "List of University of California, Berkeley faculty") UC Berkeley professors have also received the Pulitzer Prize. Alumna and professor [Susan Rasky](/wiki/Susan_Rasky "Susan Rasky") (BA 1974) won the [Polk Award](/wiki/George_Polk_Awards "George Polk Awards") for journalism in 1991. USC Professor and Berkeley alumnus [Viet Thanh Nguyen](/wiki/Viet_Thanh_Nguyen "Viet Thanh Nguyen")'s (PhD 1997) first novel *[The Sympathizer](/wiki/The_Sympathizer "The Sympathizer")* won the 2016 [Pulitzer Prize for Fiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Fiction "Pulitzer Prize for Fiction").[[284]](#cite_note-286)

#### Fiction and screenwriters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Fiction and screenwriters")]

[Irving Stone](/wiki/Irving_Stone "Irving Stone") (BA 1923) wrote the novel *[Lust for Life](/wiki/Lust_for_Life_(novel) "Lust for Life (novel)")*, which was later made into an Academy Award-winning [film of the same name](/wiki/Lust_for_Life_(1956_film) "Lust for Life (1956 film)") starring [Kirk Douglas](/wiki/Kirk_Douglas "Kirk Douglas") as [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh"). Stone also wrote *[The Agony and the Ecstasy](/wiki/The_Agony_and_the_Ecstasy_(novel) "The Agony and the Ecstasy (novel)")*, which was later made into a [film of the same name](/wiki/The_Agony_and_the_Ecstasy_(film) "The Agony and the Ecstasy (film)") starring Oscar winner [Charlton Heston](/wiki/Charlton_Heston "Charlton Heston") as [Michelangelo](/wiki/Michelangelo "Michelangelo"). [Mona Simpson](/wiki/Mona_Simpson_(novelist) "Mona Simpson (novelist)") (BA 1979) wrote the novel *[Anywhere But Here](/wiki/Anywhere_But_Here_(film) "Anywhere But Here (film)")*, which was later made into a film of the same name starring Oscar-winning actress [Susan Sarandon](/wiki/Susan_Sarandon "Susan Sarandon"). [Terry McMillan](/wiki/Terry_McMillan "Terry McMillan") (BA 1986) wrote *[How Stella Got Her Groove Back](/wiki/How_Stella_Got_Her_Groove_Back "How Stella Got Her Groove Back")*, which was later made into a film of the same name starring Oscar-nominated actress [Angela Bassett](/wiki/Angela_Bassett "Angela Bassett"). [Randi Mayem Singer](/wiki/Randi_Mayem_Singer "Randi Mayem Singer") (BA 1979) wrote the screenplay for *[Mrs. Doubtfire](/wiki/Mrs._Doubtfire "Mrs. Doubtfire")*, which starred Oscar-winning actor [Robin Williams](/wiki/Robin_Williams "Robin Williams") and Oscar-winning actress [Sally Field](/wiki/Sally_Field "Sally Field"). [Audrey Wells](/wiki/Audrey_Wells "Audrey Wells") (BA 1981) wrote the screenplay *[The Truth About Cats & Dogs](/wiki/The_Truth_About_Cats_%26_Dogs "The Truth About Cats & Dogs")*, which starred Oscar-nominated actress [Uma Thurman](/wiki/Uma_Thurman "Uma Thurman"). [James Schamus](/wiki/James_Schamus "James Schamus") (BA 1982, MA 1987, PhD 2003) collaborated on screenplays with Oscar-winning director [Ang Lee](/wiki/Ang_Lee "Ang Lee") on the Academy Award-winning movies *[Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon](/wiki/Crouching_Tiger,_Hidden_Dragon "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon")* and *[Brokeback Mountain](/wiki/Brokeback_Mountain "Brokeback Mountain")*.

#### Academy and Emmy Award winners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Academy and Emmy Award winners")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Gregory_Peck_in_Roman_Holiday_trailer_cropped.jpg/250px-Gregory_Peck_in_Roman_Holiday_trailer_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gregory_Peck_in_Roman_Holiday_trailer_cropped.jpg)

[Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck"), BA 1939, [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award "Academy Award")–winning actor

Berkeley alumni have won 22 [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") and 25 [Emmy Awards](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards"). [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck") (BA 1939), nominated for four Oscars during his career, won an Oscar for acting in *[To Kill a Mockingbird](/wiki/To_Kill_a_Mockingbird_(film) "To Kill a Mockingbird (film)")*. [Marc Norman](/wiki/Marc_Norman "Marc Norman") (BA 1962, MA 1964) won two Oscars in 1999 for co-producing and co-writing the best picture winner *[Shakespeare in Love](/wiki/Shakespeare_in_Love "Shakespeare in Love")*. [Chris Innis](/wiki/Chris_Innis "Chris Innis") (BA 1991) won the 2010 Oscar for film editing for her work on best picture winner, *[The Hurt Locker](/wiki/The_Hurt_Locker "The Hurt Locker")*. [Walter Plunkett](/wiki/Walter_Plunkett "Walter Plunkett") (BA 1923) won an Oscar for costume design (for *[An American in Paris](/wiki/An_American_in_Paris "An American in Paris")*). [Freida Lee Mock](/wiki/Freida_Lee_Mock "Freida Lee Mock") (BA 1961) and [Charles H. Ferguson](/wiki/Charles_H._Ferguson "Charles H. Ferguson") (BA 1978) have each[[285]](#cite_note-287)[[286]](#cite_note-288) won an Oscar for documentary filmmaking. Mark Berger (BA 1964) has won four Oscars for sound mixing and is an adjunct professor at UC Berkeley.[[287]](#cite_note-289) [Edith Head](/wiki/Edith_Head "Edith Head") (BA 1918), who was nominated for 34 Oscars during her career, won eight Oscars for costume design. [Joe Letteri](/wiki/Joe_Letteri "Joe Letteri") (BA 1981[[288]](#cite_note-290)) has won four Oscars for Best Visual Effects in the [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron "James Cameron") film *[Avatar](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)")* and the [Peter Jackson](/wiki/Peter_Jackson "Peter Jackson") films *[King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong_(2005_film) "King Kong (2005 film)")*, *[The Two Towers](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings:_The_Two_Towers "The Lord of the Rings: The Two Towers")*, and *[The Return of the King](/wiki/The_Lord_of_the_Rings:_The_Return_of_the_King "The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King")*.[[289]](#cite_note-291) [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Awards "Emmy Awards") winners include Jon Else (BA 1968) for cinematography; [Andrew Schneider](/wiki/Andrew_Schneider "Andrew Schneider") (BA 1973) for screenwriting; Linda Schacht (BA 1966, MA 1981), two for broadcast journalism;[[290]](#cite_note-292)[[291]](#cite_note-293) Christine Chen (dual-BA's 1990), two for broadcast journalism;[[292]](#cite_note-294) [Kathy Baker](/wiki/Kathy_Baker "Kathy Baker") (BA 1977), three for acting; Ken Milnes (BS 1977), four for broadcasting technology; and [Leroy Sievers](/wiki/Leroy_Sievers "Leroy Sievers") (BA 1977),[[293]](#cite_note-ABCNews_Sievers_2008_08_16-295) twelve for production. [Elisabeth Leamy](/wiki/Elisabeth_Leamy "Elisabeth Leamy") (BA 1989) is the recipient of thirteen [Emmy awards](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award").[[294]](#cite_note-296)[[295]](#cite_note-297)[[296]](#cite_note-298)

#### Music and entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Music and entertainment")]

Former undergraduates have participated in the contemporary music industry, such as [Grateful Dead](/wiki/Grateful_Dead "Grateful Dead") bass guitarist [Phil Lesh](/wiki/Phil_Lesh "Phil Lesh"), [the Police](/wiki/The_Police "The Police") drummer [Stewart Copeland](/wiki/Stewart_Copeland "Stewart Copeland"),[[297]](#cite_note-299) *[Rolling Stone](/wiki/Rolling_Stone "Rolling Stone")* founder [Jann Wenner](/wiki/Jann_Wenner "Jann Wenner"), [the Bangles](/wiki/The_Bangles "The Bangles") lead singer [Susanna Hoffs](/wiki/Susanna_Hoffs "Susanna Hoffs") (BA 1980), [Counting Crows](/wiki/Counting_Crows "Counting Crows") lead singer [Adam Duritz](/wiki/Adam_Duritz "Adam Duritz"), electronic music producer [Giraffage](/wiki/Giraffage "Giraffage"), [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV") correspondent [Suchin Pak](/wiki/Suchin_Pak "Suchin Pak") (BA 1997),[[298]](#cite_note-300) [AFI](/wiki/AFI_(band) "AFI (band)") musicians [Davey Havok](/wiki/Davey_Havok "Davey Havok") and [Jade Puget](/wiki/Jade_Puget "Jade Puget") (BA 1996), and solo artist [Marié Digby](/wiki/Mari%C3%A9_Digby "Marié Digby") ("[Say It Again](/wiki/Say_It_Again_(Digby_song) "Say It Again (Digby song)")"). *[People Magazine](/wiki/People_Magazine "People Magazine")* included [Third Eye Blind](/wiki/Third_Eye_Blind "Third Eye Blind") lead singer and songwriter [Stephan Jenkins](/wiki/Stephan_Jenkins "Stephan Jenkins") (BA 1987) in the magazine's list of *50 Most Beautiful People*.[[299]](#cite_note-301) Alumni have also acted in classic television series such as [Karen Grassle](/wiki/Karen_Grassle "Karen Grassle") (BA 1965) who played [Caroline Ingalls](/wiki/Caroline_Ingalls "Caroline Ingalls") in *[Little House on the Prairie](/wiki/Little_House_on_the_Prairie_(TV_series) "Little House on the Prairie (TV series)")*, [Jerry Mathers](/wiki/Jerry_Mathers "Jerry Mathers") (BA 1974) who starred in *[Leave it to Beaver](/wiki/Leave_it_to_Beaver "Leave it to Beaver")*, and [Roxann Dawson](/wiki/Roxann_Dawson "Roxann Dawson") (BA 1980) who portrayed [B'Elanna Torres](/wiki/B%27Elanna_Torres "B'Elanna Torres") on *[Star Trek: Voyager](/wiki/Star_Trek:_Voyager "Star Trek: Voyager")*.

#### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_California,_Berkeley&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Natalie_Coughlin%2C_2018_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Natalie_Coughlin%2C_2018_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Natalie_Coughlin,_2018_(cropped).jpg)

[Natalie Coughlin](/wiki/Natalie_Coughlin "Natalie Coughlin"), BA 2005, multiple gold medal-winning Olympic swimmer

Sport alumni include tennis athlete [Helen Wills Moody](/wiki/Helen_Wills_Moody "Helen Wills Moody") (BA 1925) won 31 [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis) "Grand Slam (tennis)") titles, including eight singles titles at [Wimbledon](/wiki/The_Championships,_Wimbledon "The Championships, Wimbledon"). [Tarik Glenn](/wiki/Tarik_Glenn "Tarik Glenn") (BA 1999) is a [Super Bowl XLI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLI "Super Bowl XLI") champion. [Michele Tafoya](/wiki/Michele_Tafoya "Michele Tafoya") (BA 1988) is a sports television reporter for [ABC Sports](/wiki/ABC_Sports "ABC Sports") and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN").[[300]](#cite_note-302) [Sports agent](/wiki/Sports_agent "Sports agent") [Leigh Steinberg](/wiki/Leigh_Steinberg "Leigh Steinberg") (BA 1970, JD 1973) has represented professional athletes such as [Steve Young](/wiki/Steve_Young_(American_football) "Steve Young (American football)"), [Troy Aikman](/wiki/Troy_Aikman "Troy Aikman"), and [Oscar De La Hoya](/wiki/Oscar_De_La_Hoya "Oscar De La Hoya"); Steinberg has been called the real-life inspiration[[301]](#cite_note-303) for the title character in the Oscar-winning[[302]](#cite_note-304) film *[Jerry Maguire](/wiki/Jerry_Maguire "Jerry Maguire")* (portrayed by [Tom Cruise](/wiki/Tom_Cruise "Tom Cruise")). [Matt Biondi](/wiki/Matt_Biondi "Matt Biondi") (BA 1988) won eight Olympic gold medals during his swimming career, in which he participated in three different Olympics. At the [Beijing Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics") in 2008, [Natalie Coughlin](/wiki/Natalie_Coughlin "Natalie Coughlin") (BA 2005) became the first American female athlete in modern Olympic history to win six medals in one Olympics.[[303]](#cite_note-Aron2008_08_18-305)
